Common and Uncommon Ground: A Panel Exchange

Henry Smith

Henry Smith

17 Hammond Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: Henryfsmith@cs.com

Henry Smith   This panel was summarized in a report by Jonathan Dunn, JAPA 51/3 (2003, pp. 941-955).

Henry F. Smith was the discussant at the panel “Have We Changed Our View of the Unconscious in Contemporary Clinical Work?” at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 18-19, 1998. Panelists included Jane V. Kite (chair), Sander Abend, Ronald Britton, Ernest S. Wolf, Polly Young-Eisendrath, Philip Bromberg, and Owen Renik. Smith's discussion is presented here, along with three of the panelists' responses to it.

On Borrowed Words: A Memoir of Languages. By Ilan Stavans. New York: Viking Penguin, 2001, 262 pp., $25.95.

Jacqueline Amati Mehler

Jacqueline Amati Mehler

Via Lucrezio Caro 62
00193 Rome
Italy
E-mail: Jacqueline@tiscalinet.it

Jacqueline Amati Mehler   Training and Supervising Analyst, Italian Psychoanalytical Association; President, Italian Psychoanalytical Association.

Belonging as I do to the community of “exiled polylinguals,” I was fascinated by the title of this book by Ilan Stavans and looked forward to sharing the story of his attempt to communicate the fate of words and their meaning as they accompany experiences of migration through different lands, languages, affects, and object relations.

The author left Mexico—“my illiterate Mexico, where the written word has always felt like an imposition, a foreign import” (p. 18)—at the age of twenty-four and came to New York barely knowing English. In the first chapter of the book, “Mexico Lindo,” Stavans tells us that while packing his library to leave New York for Massachusetts, he thinks that when he unpacks the books from the numbered boxes he will certainly arrange them in a different order. He wonders how much he and his library “have changed together this past decade” and what the books say about him (p. 3). And this, indeed, seems to be one of the crucial issues of On Borrowed Words. I wondered on first seeing the volume whether Stavans's narrative was mainly about his encounter with books and their authors. Or perhaps it was the story of the different languages and objects that inhabit his internal world, confronted with the perception of changing contexts as he wanders through countries and cultures, partly in the real world and partly via identification with the characters of the many books he has read. Or was it more simply the autobiography of an author in search of his own identity, permanently exiled?

The search for an identity and a place of his own is deeply interwoven with words and books. Stavans says that no matter where he travels, the only place he truly belongs is New York. Why? “The answer,” he says, is “its literature” (p. 21). For Stavans, every place in Manhattan is connected with a book and its content. Throughout On Borrowed Words

The Intertwining of Politics and Science in Psychoanalytic History

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920

Robert Wallerstein  

It is felicitous and timely that the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association has built an issue around the contentious questions underlying the subtitle of Arnold Richards's target paper, “A Plea for a Measure of Humility.” Six commentaries and two articles in response to his paper have been contributed by the editors or past editors of other major psychoanalytic journals, and this section is preceded by three plenary presentations to APsaA's national meetings, each dealing with a topic closely related to Richards's central theme. Also included are two articles that fortuitously came over the transom, so to speak, each again dealing with an aspect of this supplement's central issue.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

JAPA: The Next Half-Century

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards  

The blue-covered issues of our Fiftieth Anniversary volume provided our readers with contributions on psychoanalytic theory, psychoanalytic research, psychoanalytic practice and technique, and psychoanalytic studies of culture.1 This issue begins the first volume of JAPA's second half-century.

This issue also begins the last year of my editorship of JAPA. I am pleased to announce that Steven T. Levy has been designated editor-elect. He will begin his first five-year term in January of 2004. You will note his name on the masthead of this issue as a member of the JAPA Administrative Board. He was a member of the JAPA Editorial Board from 1999 to 2001.

For twenty-nine years, Levy has been a faculty member at Emory University, where he is currently the Bernard C. Holland Professor and Vice-Chairman of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences. He is also Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Psychoanalysis at the Morehouse School of Medicine as well as Chief of Psychiatry at both Grady Memorial Hospital and the Central Fulton Community Mental Health Center. Additionally, he is Training and Supervising Analyst and former Director at the Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute.

Levy has served on the editorial boards of the Psychoanalytic Quarterly and the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. His contributions to the psychoanalytic literature have centered on controversial issues in psychoanalytic technique, although he has also written about the treatment of acute psychosis, the combined use of psychotherapy and psychopharmacology, and the relationship between psychodynamics and neuroscience.

Within the American Psychoanalytic Association, Levy chairs the Committee on Research and Special Training (CORST), which oversees the training of academics in clinical psychoanalysis.

1We now return to our seasonal color cycle, blue for winter, green for spring, yellow for summer, and red for fall.5

[[RunningHead]]

The Contemporary Psychoanalyst at Work: Research—Continuing Dialogues †

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser  

The three papers in this issue that anchor JAPA's commitment to feature psychoanalytic research regularly (see JAPA: The Next Half-Century, p. 5), extend and enrich themes expressed in papers published here during the past year in “Varieties of Psychoanalytic Research” (50/2) and “Psychoanalytic Practice and Research: New Explorations and Implications” (50/3).

Contributions to the latter emphasized key challenges we now face in bringing contemporary research approaches to bear, meaningfully, upon psychoanalysis, so as to study rigorously our conceptual frame-works and clinical practices. Karlen Lyons-Ruth (1998) clearly articu-lates these challenges: “Psychoanalysis is faced with the challenge of altering its culture to sustain parallel and mutually renewing scientific and clinical traditions. This will involve a need for the more stream-lined and technical versions of the constructs necessary for research while continuing to support the richer and more evocative language needed to capture the essence of psychoanalytic work in vivo” (p. 130).

In order to engage successfully with the perspectives and the empirical approaches of related disciplines, we must be prepared to sharpen the meanings of relevant constructs (theoretical and clinical), and be ready to eschew such obfuscating polarizations as qualitative or quantitative research, objective versus subjective data, descriptive or

[[RunningHead]]

Is There Life Without Mother? Psychoanalysis, Biography, Creativity. By Leonard Shengold. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2000, 232 pp., $42.50.

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber

258 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: pschwaber@wesleyan.edu

Paul Schwaber   faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Professor of Letters, Wesleyan University.

The heart of this book is a long, nuanced study of Anthony Trollope, a Victorian novelist whose works, like Dickens's, were often read aloud at family hearths for an evening's entertainment. The son of a bullying, unbalanced father and a socially winning but inattentive mother, both of whom were writers, young Anthony seemed oafish and dunce-like at school. Withdrawn from Harrow when his barrister father declared bankruptcy, he found a niche in the British Postal service, where he worked for thirty-three years, in increasingly responsible positions, before retiring to focus solely on his writing. He helped to reform the postal service of England and Ireland, is credited with having invented the common letterbox, and is said to have written and published more words than any other English novelist. Thus uncommonly and admirably, he contributed to the communicability, ease, and closeness of his time. Such was hardly, however, his own experience growing up. Shengold, scrutinizing Trollope's posthumously published autobiography, his fiction, and other sources, demonstrates that the prodigious novelist—he published sixty-seven books in all—suffered unremitting abuse, misery, and deprivation in childhood. How he accomplished all that he did, at what cost, and through what psychological deployments engages Shengold, who sees Trollope as a survivor of soul murder.

For several decades now, Leonard Shengold (1988, 1989, 1999) has pondered soul murder and its effects, the cruel process by which love and joy are killed in a human soul. The descriptive term surfaced in von Feuerbach's study of the unfortunate foundling Kaspar Hauser Ibsen, and Freud's Dr. Schreber (1989, pp. 14-23). Shengold brings compassionate psychoanalytic discernment and capaciousness to it, taking note of signs of long developmental ordeals and recognizing the devastating dynamics wrought by too much childhood abuse and deprivation—experiences of seduction, brutality, and desolation too intense and sustained for a child to bear. Any child's experience involves degrees of seduction and injury. Parents love, touch, and separate out. Frustration attends psychic growth. Inborn capacities vary.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in Toronto: Historical Perspectives. By Douglas Frayn. Toronto: Ash Productions, 2000, 206 pp., $25.00

James Naiman

James Naiman

2156 Sherbrooke Street West
Suite 6
Montreal, QC H3H IG7
Canada
Email: Cpsqeb@qc.aira.com

James Naiman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Canadian Institute of Psychoanalysis; Associate Professor of Psychology, McGill University.

In Toronto, as in other cities, psychoanalytic history began when an analyst came to settle in the area. In this instance, it was Alan Parkin, who came to the Canadian city in 1954. Today, nearly fifty years later, there are approximately 150 analysts in the Toronto Society, of whom thirty are training analysts. There are at present thirty candidates.

Douglas Frayn, the author of Psychoanalysis in Toronto, was a member of the first class to take its training entirely in the city. That was in 1969, and, in a class of nine, all were male and, with the exception of Charles Hanly, medically qualified. Fourteen years later, in 1983, there was finally a class in which women outnumbered men, and by 1999 half the class was nonmedical.

Frayn includes material written by seventeen colleagues. The second analyst in Toronto was Irving Schiffer, who has this to say: “we did a lot of fighting among ourselves … plenty of dirty laundry and at times political farce” (p. 22). He happens to have been Jeffrey Masson's analyst. Could his analysand have identified with him?

Conflicts between society and institute resulted in the creation of the Faculty, institute teachers and committee members who are not training analysts. Mary Kay O'Neil speaks of “a quickly squelched trial to allow all analysts to analyze candidates, as happens in the Quebec French Institute” (p. 35). This is incorrect insofar as Quebec is concerned. According to the current roster of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society, only sixty-three of ninety-three members of the French Society are deemed competent to analyze candidates.

In 1970, Ruth Easser, now deceased, became the first woman training analyst in Toronto, having obtained this status at Columbia.

A Universe of Consciousness: How Matter Becomes Imagination. By Gerald M. Edelman and Giulio Tononi. New York: Basic Books, 2000, 274 pp., $17.00 paperback.

Dwarakanath Rao

Dwarakanath Rao

555 East William
Suite 241
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
Fax: 734-994-9107
E-mail: Dgrao@umich.edu

Dwarakanath Rao   Faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, University of Michigan.

This book begins with the wonderment expressed by the neurophysiologist Sherrington: “When I turn my gaze skyward I see the flattened dome of the sky and the sun's brilliant disc and a hundred other visible things underneath it. What are the steps which bring this about? A pencil of light from the sun enters the eye and is focussed there on the retina. It gives rise to a change, which in turn travels to the nerve layer at the top of the brain. The whole chain of these events, from the sun to the top of my brain, is physical. Each step is an electrical reaction. But now there succeeds a change wholly unlike any that led up to it, and wholly inexplicable by us. A visual scene presents itself to the mind: I see the dome of the sky and the sun in it, and a hundred other visual things beside. In fact, I perceive a picture of the world around me” (p. 1).

Edelman and Tononi argue that this rendering of the world into subjective experience is ultimately explicable, but only by modern neuroscience. In the process, they take on the daunting tasks of defining and explaining, in the role of newly inspired frontiersmen, a host of issues—memory, thinking, conscious and unconscious experience, the idea of self, mind/body, and, above all, natural selection and its profound shaping of brain function.

The book is beautifully organized, aptly metaphorical, and astonishingly clear, despite its being quite technical in places.

1030

Treatment, Supervision, and Language

Learning From our Mistakes: Beyond Dogma In Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy. By Patrick Casement. New York: Guilford Press, 2002, 170 pp. $25.00.

Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

206 South Main Street
Suite 238
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
Fax: 734-996-5852
E-mail: Mshulmanphd@lycos.com

Michael Shulman   Adjunct Associate Professor of Psychology, Madonna University; advanced candidate, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute.

Offered by the analyst to help free a patient, analytic interpretation instead frequently forecloses and imprisons. This is Patrick Casement's thesis in this new collection of papers, a follow-up to the work published in the U.S. in 1991 as Learning from the Patient. The unwitting authoritarianism of the analyst is Casement's principal target of criticism. His greatest concern is the risk of creating compliant and conforming patients through analysis.

With the exception of Freud, whose authoritarianism in technique Casement clearly documents, the authorities whose authoritarianism is so to be avoided are unnamed. They haunt his concerns, however, in the fashion of ghosts, or those “dead generations” whose history, Marx (1852) wrote, “weighs like a nightmare on the brain of the living” (p. 15). Implicitly, Casement's suggestions conjure certain modern Kleinians who tend to make statements of interpretations, in a way that does not appear to express uncertainty or admit the possibility of error. These analysts speak most often in intonations of solid certitude; they appear neither to ask questions in sessions nor to utter speculations in the presence of patients. (Puzzlingly, Casement twice suggests in this new book that analysts who need to speak with certainty may be less objectionable as analysts conducting training analyses than as analysts for patients who are not pursuing analytic training.)

To my mind, the ghosts of the analyst's authority, and of the negative potential of authoritarianism, are bothersome ghosts with which American analysts, too, have been struggling for some time. I believe, in fact, that a number of Americans have made more headway here than has Casement.

1049

David Wolitzky Replies

David Wolitzky

David Wolitzky

New York, NY 10012
E-mail: Dwolitzky@aol.com

David Wolitzky  

August 13, 2003. I appreciate the opportunity for a brief reply to Joseph Schachter's rebuttal of some of the comments I offered in my review of his book. He lists five points in his rebuttal. Because I do not strongly disagree with points 2 through 5, I will limit my remarks mainly to his first point.

When I stated that I was not clear what Schachter meant by stating that a focus on the here-and-now transference “may throw light, by extrapolation into the past, on the meaning of earlier experiences, including those of childhood, rather than the other way around, “I italicized the last phrase because I thought that Schachter was making a spurious distinction between interpreting the past in terms of the present and interpreting the present in terms of the past. In other words, the here-and-now transference, or the “patient-analyst interaction,” as Schachter prefers to call it, can be used to try to shed light on the past, as Schachter notes, but an understanding of past interactions with significant figures can also shed light on present relationships. I see no contradiction in working from both directions in promoting the patient's sense of personal history, nor am I aware of any evidence that consistently starting from the present produces superior therapeutic results. Further, it is difficult for me to see that one or the other preference in clinical technique requires a radical revision of the theory of transference. I assume that most analysts regularly work from both directions in linking the present and the past. In arriving at a so-called reconstruction, for example, we generally start from a dynamic theme that emerges in the present analytic interaction and work backward to infer its developmental origins.

1100

Common and Uncommon Ground: Panelist's Response

Ernest Wolf

Ernest Wolf

180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60601
Fax: 847-446-3184
E-mail: Eswolf@3b.com

Ernest Wolf  

Smith begins his brilliant discussion by reminding us of Wallerstein's observation that all of us psychoanalysts share a common ground of certain assumptions about the human mind and its functioning. And while the bulk of his essay highlights our differences with each other so as to elucidate and illuminate what we actually do in our clinical work with our analysands, Smith ends on the theme of the commonality of all of us “fishing in the same waters,” even though we sound so different from each other.

Why do we sound so different from each other when we really talk about closely related ideas, and what does this mean? Much depends on the questions that we are trying to answer. When we are attempting to clarify our basic principles and processes we often use different words, almost different languages, to label ideas and concepts that are essentially alike. For example, whether I call a fear motivated by the need not to know something a defensive negation, a denial, a disavowal, a disowning, a renunciation, an abjuration, or a repression, the meaning of all these words is approximately the same when used in the context of a psychoanalytic view of dynamic mental events. As an innovative theoretician describing some new insights, of course, I might well be disposed to cover up my insecurity over their acceptance or rejection by asserting my ideas via a new and special word with a clearly spelled out definition and an absoluteness that does not permit synonyms. Are any of us really free of succumbing, at least occasionally, to a linguistic declaration of our greatness that helps us hide some secret conviction of really being flawed? How else can we impress our colleagues, and, especially our selfs, in the Common Room? No wonder there are various schools of psychoanalysis that often treat each other as if they were enemies. Narcissism? ‘Nuff said.

Another source of conceptual fuzziness comes from the use of metaphors. The mature Freud (1926) knew this very well, but realized that metaphors could not be avoided: “In psychology we can only describe things by the help of analogies. There is nothing peculiar in this; it is the case elsewhere as well. But we have constantly to keep changing these analogies, for none of them lasts us long enough” (p. 195). But already in 1893 he had recognized the need and the limitation: “I am making use here of a number of similes, all of which have only a very limited resemblance to my subject and which, moreover, are incompatible with one another.

Common and Uncommon Ground: Panelist's Response

Polly Young-Eisendrath

Polly Young-Eisendrath

195 Calais Road
Worcester, VT 05682
E-mail: Pollye@adelphia.net

Polly Young-Eisendrath  

First I would like to thank Jonathan Dunn for his lucid and accurate summary of my presentation, and Harry Smith for his cogent comments and questions. Before I respond in greater detail to Smith's comments, I would like to mention one general point about both commentaries: the explicit contrast that was drawn between my Jungian model of unconscious complexes and Philip Bromberg's interpersonal model of dissociated self-states is misleading. Bromberg and I come from very similar theoretical and clinical assumptions. We both embrace the belief that human subjectivity has multiple centers with conflicting and disparate motivations, images, and impulses that are only loosely related— if at all—to ego consciousness. For us, the clinical problem is not what keeps these things unconscious, but how they can become associated with consciousness at all, so that the patient can experience them as conflicting states of subjectivity, rather than as aspects of objective reality.

Smith asked in his commentary why “making the unconscious conscious makes a difference.” My response is that it permits a person more subjective freedom to live intentionally rather than reactively. Smith says that “some things are kept unconscious because of the degree to which the person would suffer were it not so.” By contrast, I would say that many emotional habits are unconscious because we develop in such a way as to respond to certain important cues and stimuli automatically, without conscious awareness. Smith repeatedly asks each of the panelists one question: What keeps the unconscious unconscious? I believe this is a wrong question because it makes an a priori assumption that something keeps something else out of awareness. Freud's model of the dynamic unconscious—repressed conflicts kept out of awareness by a censor and then defended against by the ego—is not, in my view, comprehensive or clinically useful enough to be our primary model for the role played by the unconscious workings of emotional memories in everyday life and psychopathology.

Arlene Richards

Arlene Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128

Arlene Richards   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society; Fellow, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

In this scholarly and closely reasoned book, Arnold Davidson considers the origin of a conceptual schema that created three institutions: psychiatry as a medical discipline, psychoanalysis as a theory of the mind and a treatment modality for mental illness, and sexuality as a science of desire. He elegantly shows how this schema—the medical diagnosis and treatment of actions, thoughts, and feelings—developed in the nineteenth century, replacing the idea that madness was a theological problem. Following Foucault on this path, he extends historical epistemology to assert that concepts (in the philosophical sense) are not fixed and given Platonic ideals, but rather pieces of a world view that changes with historical circumstance and, he implies, with the evolution of thought. His book is timely now that the paths of psychiatry and psychoanalysis seem to be diverging—the former toward medicalization, while the latter develops into a talking cure administered by psychologists, social workers, and others. In our time, the concept of mental illness is developing along a bifurcation between (on the one side) dispensers of medications for anxiety, depression, hyperactivity, schizophrenia, and other specific symptom constellations, and (on the other) patient and painstakingly slow exploration of a person's thoughts and feelings with the goal of achieving the most adaptive and least painful compromises among one's wishes, fears, moral ideals, and relationships with others. To see where we are going, it may help to see where we have been. From this point of view, Davidson's history of our concepts and their relations is valuable for analysts.

1385

I lived on a farm where animals—horses, cows, sheep, pigs, dogs, cats—foaled, calved, gave birth to piglets, puppies, kittens; chicken, ducks, geese and turkeys laid eggs and brooded them until their young hatched. The summers and autumns of my childhood were filled with these events, which we followed with great curiosity and interest. Daily talk centered around fertility and procreation. There was also constant talk about women's deliveries and pregnancies. My mother gave birth to twins, and this was also for me an impressive and magnificent event [p. 128].

This is a small volume out of Finland, a country whose mythology is ruled by powerful and sometimes malevolent mother figures, and whose society provides at least nine months' maternity leave, beginning one month before birth. It is a volume about daughters and their relationship to their mothers, and in it, in her passion and simple writing, Reenkola seeks to remind us of what has been repressed in phallocentric psychoanalytic theory.

The thrust of this work is that a woman's sense of her inner space, her pleasure in it, and her knowledge of its creativity have been obscured by past theory, and that women themselves (including women analysts), aware of their fecundity and specialness but very protective of it, have tended to obscure or veil

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Contemporary Psychoanalyst at Work: Theory and Practice †

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

Many articles in this issue can be viewed as continuing a discussion begun in JAPA 50/3 (“Psychoanalysis: Practice and Technique”) of what North American psychoanalysts are doing, how they are conceptualizing what they are doing, and what their concerns are. Lawrence Friedman, introducing the 50/3 papers, discerned a general retreat from theory. He speculated that theory, having become exhausted by “the great American theory project,” was later splintered by deconstructive challenges from interpersonal and relational perspectives (see the Reis panel report in this issue). Friedman pondered the consequences of movement away from a theory that has been traditionally structured around concepts of resistance and has defined what we seek from, and do with, patients. Will we lose core concepts that define what we do that is different from other disciplines and that enable us to talk to one another? Reading the papers in this issue suggests that analysts are still concerned with the relationship of theory to technique and that they have not abandoned the traditional vocabulary of psycho-analysis, even as they are responding to the changing environments in which they work. Yet these papers also reveal that some things have indeed changed.

Analysts are still concerned about the relationship of theory to practice, although the range of compared theories is broader and the conclusions postmodern. Richard Almond, for example, examines analytic data from three theoretical perspectives; he compares his own clinical material, understood from multiple theoretical perspectives, to the published material of a neo-Kleinian (Betty Joseph) and a relation-alist (Lewis Aron).

I began to read Psychoanalytic Conversations two weeks after Stephen Mitchell's sudden and untimely death. So, when I found Mitchell's name among the ten interviews in this book, it was an unexpected surprise. I initially intended to read the book from beginning to end, but I instantly turned to the Mitchell interview. Although it had taken place in 1998, I had the uncanny thought (and wish) that Mitchell were returning to give some final word, to sum things up, to say some last goodbye.

Time was, at least in some psychoanalytic institutes, when to maintain that the analyst should foster the therapeutic alliance was about as controversial as maintaining that the office should contain a couch. No more. As this volume demonstrates, there are serious questions about the utility of the alliance concept, both as an element of clinical theory and as an influence on technique. The book is the product of two panel presentations at meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, and the ninth in their published “Workshop Series”; it includes five individual papers and two discussions, with introductory comments by the editor.

In the opening paper, Sander Abend traces the historical development of the concept, beginning with Freud's designation of the “unobjectionable positive transference,” which enabled the analyst to secure the patient's cooperation. Subsequent theorists—Sterba, Zetzel, and Greenson, in particular—went well beyond Freud in separating off a mature, rational part of the patient's ego that could enter into a working partnership with the analyst. Abend gives a balanced account of these developments, but he is more in agreement with other writers, especially Friedman and Brenner, who have questioned whether patients' (and analysts') motivations are really so separable into the rational/cooperative and irrational/defensive. Better, say these writers, to maintain a consistent focus on searching for unconscious factors than to engage in “alliance-building” techniques that may limit the depth of exploration and may serve the analyst's needs more than the patient's.

Among the other panelists, Axel Hoffer is closest to Abend in his skepticism toward the therapeutic alliance. He argues that the analyst who ventures beyond the basic task of enhancing free association and elucidating conflict may, in seeking to “build the alliance,” actually interfere with expressions of deeper conflict and impose a form of compliance on the patient.

Gerald Adler is also critical of the alliance concept, but for a particular group of patients: those with borderline and narcissistic personality disorders. For these patients, it is the selfobject transference that carries the treatment for a long time, and that may be mistaken for a true alliance in periods of stability.

October 1, 2002. I am sorry that Anna Aragno thinks my review does not do justice to her work. I will take most of her points in turn, but will mostly restrict myself to general points with examples, rather than offering a comprehensive rebuttal of each point, which would surely be of interest to only Aragno and myself.

I would like to state, first of all, that I am sorry she feels that the review leaves an acidic aftertaste “due to the subtle, yet unmistakable, flavor of ‘seniority’ with which it is imbibed.” I assume that the author means imbued (rather than imbibed), in which case I sincerely apologize for any air of condescension—probably caused by exactly the sort of suggested clarification I have just made! My students would probably agree that I can be pedantic in judging writing for its expression, as well as for what is being said. The comments on style did not, however, come from any sense whatever of seniority in relation to Aragno; I completed my Ph.D. at about the same time as she seems to have completed hers, and she may well be a more experienced clinician and writer. Furthermore, she knows far more philosophy than I do, which is much of what her book concerns. My commentary was aimed not from any lofty position at all, but from that of a peer struggling with similar issues within a neighboring discipline—I hope not, as Aragno asserts, from a different planet.

Let us get to specifics.

This slim volume is a contribution to the as yet scanty psychoanalytic literature relating to lesbians. The seventeen essays, written by both analysts and nonanalysts, are an interdisciplinary collection exploring sexuality between women from a variety of perspectives. The essays emphasize the diversity rather than the unity of this group of individuals, defined as a group only by their gender (female) and their desire (for another woman or women). Some of the essays contain rich clinical material drawn from the treatment of individual women. There is an excellent bibliography.

No single theoretical psychoanalytic frame is accepted by all the authors, and some dismiss all psychoanalytic theories. The one point of theoretical unity among these papers is the rejection of theories that consider homosexuality or homoeroticism to be evidence of abnormality. This book can be regarded as a partially successful effort to correct prior bias. Some of the authors regard analytic neutrality with suspicion, and advocate that analysts adopt a validating stance with regard to their lesbian patients' sexuality. Some of the chapters are weakened by the polemical intensity of their arguments, and should be read with an understanding of the feeling of outrage evoked by the historical psychoanalytic position that any same-sex object choice must be severely pathological.

The book is divided into four sections. However, the essays do not lend themselves easily to this attempt at categorization.

1395

Nothing prepares the reader for Daniel Widlöcher's remarkable collection better than a trip to the Marché aux Puces, the Flea Market, on the outskirts of Paris. Board the Métro somewhere say around St-Germain-des-Prés or Odéon, and take the “4” out to Porte de Clignancourt. The trip can feel rather long the first time you do it, as though you might drop off the end of the earth into one “no-fly zone” or another. But it's full of surprise. Young lovers come and go, looking as though the next stop would surely be the consummate one. Bands of Roma musicians move from car to car, enchanting some, annoying others. Flesh tones change from peach and rose to olive and coffee. Even the aromas change. The scents of the Left Bank—fresh bread and Camembert and raspberries—turn to sesame, vanilla, and tangerine. Once out of the Métro, moving through the shacks of T-shirt vendors and card-readers, you will think “New York! Canal Street!” That momentary dislocation will only enhance your deepening wonder as you move onto the rue des Rosiers, the market's main street. You will find more than two thousand vendors of everything from vintage chic to antiquities.

1404

A psychoanalytic novel in verse? Come again? Wait, don't leave—this is the best psychoanalytic novel in verse I have ever read. Okay, the only one. But it is breathtakingly good. Except for those whose masochistic streaks have been insufficiently analyzed, I would predict that all readers of this journal will enjoy it immensely. So don't be put off by the poetry—read this book!

The novel takes place in the 1960s, in a psychiatric institution fictionally moved eastward to Tennessee. The seven sections describe seven sessions of each of seven analyses, including three training analyses; two characters take the role of patient in one section, and analyst in another. Why seven? Maybe the week of the Creation story, with no day of rest? Shevrin takes us inside the mind of each patient and analyst, writing perceptively and movingly about the complexities of their work together. Each section enthralls with its own deftly depicted drama. But several unifying strands link each analysis to a larger whole, adding cohesion, mutually reflecting perspectives, and (all too often) a dollop of craziness to the analytic enterprise. Shevrin explains that “The politics surrounding [the search for a new director of research for the institution] weave in and out of the seven psychoanalyses that comprise the main action” (p. xix).

“The purpose of poetry,” according to poet Paul Muldoon (2002), “is to help us make sense of who we are.” At a time when psychoanalysts are struggling with basic questions about who we are—in relation to each other, to our patients, to nonanalytic colleagues—can poetry help? Diane Ackerman, a widely published poet, essayist, and naturalist, has written a collection of poems about her experience of being in analysis. This was an unconventional analysis by most standards; it was conducted primarily by telephone, and included the use of her poems. Yet it contains within it touchstones that will readily feel familiar to analyst and analysand: intense curiosity about the analyst, grief and longing at the end of sessions, erotic fantasy, profound relief at feeling understood, the extraordinary emotional absorption of the analytic engagement.

At its core, Origami Bridges explores the relationship between poet and analyst. The poet engages the analyst. She shares her feelings, fantasies, worries, and also her poems. The analyst engages the poet.

1430

As editor of JAPA for the last ten years but, equally, as a writer, a critic, and a contributor to the theoretical and organizational developments in psychoanalysis, Arnold Richards looks out over a broad landscape, well beyond the confines of the American Psychoanalyion. Hd landscape, well beyond the confines of the American Psychoanalytic Associate does not limit himself to technical issues or specific clinical theoretical questions concerning the American or any other psychoanalytic group. Instead he enlarges his perimeter to include moves and adjustments within the broader theoretical and clinical psychoanalytic scene.

Richards travels through several decades, actually through the greater part of our psychoanalytic century, observing the changes brought about within American psychoanalytic groups, mostly by analysts who found refuge in the United States after the Second World War. He further documents the key developments that in recent decades have contributed to what can be seen as a paradigm shift in psychoanalysis—the move from an emphasis primarily on drive/conflict to a focus on relational issues.

121

July 18, 2003. Lee Jaffe's first complaint concerns my assertion that he separates the reality of training (indirect countertransference) from the fantasies it elicits for analyst and patient. As I re-read his paper, I find abundant evidence to support my assertion. For example, Jaffe writes that “it is possible that such requirements, being external influences, have been underemphasized in deference to concerns about intrapsychic phenomena …” (p. 837). Here Jaffe makes a sharp distinction between training requirements and the intrapsychic. In another instance he asserts that “indirect countertransference reactions are distinct from direct countertransference reactions, which are based on the patient's inner object world, because they introduce a complication into the analytic process that lies outside the patient-analyst dyad.” Here again he makes a clear distinction between “external influences” and the transference-countertransference field. While Jaffe suggests in his letter that he and I are saying essentially the same thing, I think I am saying something quite different. I argue that aspects of training, including institute requirements, are drawn into an established transference-countertransference field and used defensively—by candidate and patient. In contrast, Jaffe argues that training requirements cause certain countertransference reactions.

Jaffe asserts that I have committed a “category error.” I am not sure exactly what a “category error” is, but, as I understand his concern, he believes that I have failed to understand that he separated “theoretical proposition[s]” from “actual analytic work” in his discussion of countertransference. In other words, as best as I can understand, Jaffe believes that theoretically it is possible to speak of “indirect countertransference reactions as a separate reality,” even if we do not treat them as such in the consulting room. I find this position problematic. First, I do not see how one can so readily distinguish theory from practice. Practice is invariably bound by theory. Second, at least as I read them, Jaffe's clinical examples are consistent with his theoretical position: in reporting on his clinical work, he treats institute training requirements as a separate reality that intrudes into his analytic work.

P.O. Box 845
Wilmington, VT 05363
E-Mail: Danimon2@sover.net

Daniel Shapiro   Faculty, Columbia Psychoanalytic Center for Training and Research.

This collection of twenty-one relatively brief essays offers a radical postmodern and interdisciplinary critique of what are alleged to be the mindsets, clinical practices, and institutional lives of an increasing number of psychoanalysts. In a postmodern world seen increasingly as multiple and fragmented, the systems of knowledge humans create constitute the only source of meaning. There are no absolutes and no universals; no level of understanding or discourse is more basic than others. Since there is no agreed-upon methodology for determining the validity of our theoretical propositions, there is no consensus strategy for deciding who possesses knowledge.

Thus Haya Oakley, in her contribution to this collection (“What is a Psychoanalyst?”), underscores the great diversity in our notions of the unconscious, of its nature, structure, and possible dynamics. She notes that “We all claim to work with ‘repression’ and ‘resistance,’ yet have different ways of identifying what they mean and what one ‘does’ with their clinical expressions.

[[RunningHead]]

The Politics of Psychoanalysis

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

The associate editors of JAPA, wishing to honor Arnold D. Richards as he concludes his ten-year term as editor, contemplated a fitting tribute. The key elements we sought were that the tribute involve JAPA; that it highlight Arnold's abiding interest in historiography in general, and the American Psychoanalytic Association's history in particular; and that it exemplify the qualities that he has brought to the journal during the decade of his watch. Arnold's tenure at JAPA has been characterized by openness and inclusion, welcoming dialogue, and even debate, without fear of controversy.

A JAPA supplement, then, seemed the appropriate vehicle in which to combine these elements. Arnold's paper—“A Plea for a Measure of Humility”—forms the supplement's nidus, generating reactions, commentaries, and perspectives from discussants representing the diversity of our profession. Invited to respond were the editors or past editors of several leading psychoanalytic journals: Jay Greenberg and Donnel Stern (former and current editors of Contemporary Psychoanalysis), Arnold Cooper (former North American editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis), Theodore Shapiro (former editor of JAPA), Owen Renik and Henry Smith (former and current editors of Psychoanalytic Quarterly), Eva Lester (editor of the Canadian Journal of Psychoanalysis), and Neil Altman and Jody Davies (editors of Psychoanalytic Dialogues). So eager were these discussants to join the debate that two of the responses exceeded the limits of JAPA's usual target paper / commentaries format. Those contributions—by Smith and by Altman and Davies—appear as free-standing papers after the six briefer commentaries (the second of these papers has itself generated a spirited response).

Opening the issue and setting the stage are plenaries by Robert Pyles, Richard P. Fox, and Owen Renik, each of which touches on the overall topic of the supplement: the politics of psychoanalysis.

Politics is often a taboo subject in psychoanalytic discourse.

Zvi Lothane

Zvi Lothane

1435 Lexington Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Zvi Lothane   Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

Sigmund Freud is in the class of thinkers like Aristotle, Kant, Aquinas, and Nietzsche, each of whom created a universe of meaning and a perennial legacy. Just as the works have given rise to a body of studies, so Freud studies, an ongoing endeavor of preservation, presentation, and interpretation of his ideas, has filled shelf after shelf with books and periodicals. As one of such thinkers and teachers Freud not only created a climate of opinion: he collected a body of knowledge about humankind and crafted the conceptual tools to interpret it. As the greatest psychologist since Aristotle, Freud set forth a new theory of mind and gave mental health professionals a new theory of the cause and cure of mental disorders.

Since early in the 1990s, psychoanalysis has been the target of mounting attacks from various quarters, coming to a head with the historical Freud exhibit, Sigmund Freud: Conflict and Culture, organized in 1998 by the Library of Congress and the Freud Museum in London and sent on tour to a number of cities here and in Europe. The two books reviewed here are collections commemorating that exhibit. They do not engage in polemics but rather are works reaffirming Freud's legacy. The essays, roundtable discussions, and interviews contained in these two excellent anthologies convey a great deal of information, are multi-faceted and highly readable, and provide food for thought for both professionals and laypersons interested in psychoanalysis.

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place, 5th Floor

E-mail: Donaldmoss@mindspring.com

Donald Moss   Faculty, New York University Psychoanalytic Institute.

The collection of conceptually diverse and politically progressive essays gathered together in Rethinking Psychoanalysis and the Homosexualities marks an important moment in psychoanalytic history. We can finally sense within our professional boundaries an intensifying interdiction against both formal and casual expressions of homophobia. Concomitant with that interdiction, norms have changed. Within psychoanalysis, we can no longer burden the category “homosexuality” with an assumption of psychopathology.

Rethinking Psychoanalysis and the Homosexualities provides us with an opportunity to consider the determinants of the remarkable transformation in the relations between the two protagonists of its title. We have tended to categorize “homosexuality” either as an exceptional condition warranting an etiological account, or as an explicitly clinical entity. This being so, we have been permitted, even compelled, to ask questions like: “What is it?” “How does it come about?” “What does it imply?” Such questions—and the norms that support them—can no longer be assumed neutral. Regarding “homosexuality,” then, we no longer know the limits and contents of our field of observation and study. In grappling with this question, psychoanalysis reluctantly, but necessarily, grapples with the philosophical problems of postmodernism.

Beginning, perhaps, with Freud's “Three Essays on Sexuality” (1905), the classificatory project of psychoanalysis once came to us as though the product of an incomplete, though nominally disinterested, science. Now, instead of seeing evidence of disinterest in our founding taxonomies, we see signs of special interest—the shaping forces of norm, prejudice, and ideology.

The View from Inside, The View from Outside

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue
Suite 2220
Chicago, IL 60611

Bonnie Litowitz   Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Rush Medical School. Recipient of the junior Heinz Hartmann Award of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, 2003.

It might not appear at first glance that the work of the late French social scientist Pierre Bourdieu has much to offer psychoanalysis. In what follows, I would make the case that reading Bourdieu can reorient in significant ways our thinking about sociocultural contextuality and about how language functions. Traditionally, psychoanalytic theory has presented itself as concerned with inner or psychic reality resulting from each individual's path of deviation from norms that hold true for all in our species. Considerations of social or cultural relations have not been central to psychoanalytic theorizing. We are more apt to look for biological ties to related species than to explore social or cultural distinctions within our own. Our search for a universal, species-specific base for human experience would seem to preclude the usefulness of findings from studies of local variations among human groups.

I suggest that the time is ripe to reexamine the role of culture in our theories, not only because of the external realities of the world we live in, but also because a reflective awareness of sociocultural context is noticeably missing in our developmental theories. I believe that Bourdieu can help us to understand how that context is constitutive of inner reality, since this is the question he addresses in his work on practice (1977, 1990)—not how the individual can become embedded in a sociocultural context, but how the sociocultural context is embedded in the individual.

A second focus of Bourdieu's work is based in a broad critique of structural linguistics, which he claims has extracted language from its cultural context and objectified it, thereby obscuring its functioning in social relations of power, dominance, and violence. Language and Symbolic Power (1991) is a collection of Bourdieu's poststructural analyses of the structuralist tradition that begins in Saussure and extends to Lévi-Strauss, Chomsky, Austin, and Habermas (among others). Bourdieu's concept of symbolic power is imbricated in his expansion of Marx's Kapital

[[RunningHead]]

Is Free Association Still Fundamental?

Virginia McDermott

Virginia McDermott

821 Marquette Avenue
Suite 1725
Minneapolis, MN 55402
Email: Mcder001@tc.umn.edu

Virginia McDermott  

Thirty-two years ago, when the last panel on free association addressed this core psychoanalytic concept, the title of today's panel would have been inconceivable. Framing the question that generated this panel, Axel Hoffer, panel moderator and chair, noted the changes in psychoanalytic thought and practice that led him to wonder whether analysts today questioned the centrality of free association. Because of contemporary emphases on a two-person psychology, the irreducible subjectivity of the analyst, and the here and now, was the primacy of free association being displaced? Replaced? Expanded?

Hoffer set the historical primacy of free association as a unique tool of investigation, both deceptively simple and infinitely complex, in fin de siècle Vienna, where academic psychologists and psychiatrists were exploring the promises of associations as a tool for unlocking secrets of how the mind worked. Freud, Jung, and Ferenczi debated not only the role of free association in overcoming resistances, but also the way in which associations themselves could function as resistances. In a milieu in which doctors gave patients answers, Freud listened to patients to discover the answers. Unprecedented candor and honesty lay at the heart of this method.

Hoffer, turning to the German word Freud himself preferred, freier Einfall (what freely falls into your mind), noted that the person associating, minimizing self-criticism as much as possible, waits for the thoughts to “fall in.” In the analytic situation, the evenly hovering attention of the analyst is as important as the analysand's free associations. Does this inherently imply a two-person psychology? Or is it more like parallel play?

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Philadelphia, May 18, 2002. Panelists: Axel Hoffer (Chair), Peter Fonagy, Anton Kris, Lucy LaFarge, Alan Skolnikoff.

[[RunningHead]]

Partnering with the Neurosciences

Linda Mayes

Linda Mayes

230 South Frontage Road
New Haven, CT 06520-7900
Fax: 203-785-7926
Email: Linda.mayes@yale.edu

Linda Mayes  

The articles in this issue each deal with aspects of the enduring dialectic between body and mind, an interface that has defined many controversies at the core of critiques of psychoanalysis, from within and without. Psychoanalysts often point to Freud's early grounding in neurology, his interest in the most basic aspects of biology and endowment, and especially the scientific agenda he advanced in his abandoned Project as evidence for the compatibility of psychoanalytic theory and practice with contemporary brain science. Particularly for child analysts, the close relationship between development of the body and of the representational world places issues of body and mind not only in close theoretical proximity but in lively conversation in every clinical session. In the last decade, the mind-body duality has increasingly been discussed in the guise of the relevance of neuroscience—or the brain and cognitive sciences—for psychoanalysis. Many analysts concerned with psychoanalytic models of the mind have underscored the relevance of recent advances in the understanding of the neural circuitry of such basic processes as memory, stress regulation and response to trauma, and emotional processing. A new journal—Neuro-Psychoanalysis—and a number of internationally sponsored conferences on the interface of neuroscience and psychoanalysis speak to the growing interest among psychoanalysts in the new brain and cognitive sciences. Conversely, neuroscientists have expressed the hope that those sciences may offer ways to reinvigorate the scholarship on the mind-brain interface that was so central to the beginnings of psychoanalysis.

It is indeed true that there have been dramatic advances in the last ten to twenty years in our understanding of the complexity of brain functioning and brain development. Contemporary working theories of learning, for example, reveal the brain as much more dynamic than heretofore understood.

745

Analytic Training and Indirect Countertransference

Lee Jaffe

Lee Jaffe

3262 Holiday Court, Suite 210
La Jolla, CA 92037
Email: LeeJaffe@Compuserve.com

Lee Jaffe  

April 29, 2003. I read with pleasure Joshua Ehrlich's recent article on the psychoanalytic training experience (JAPA 51/1). In an effort to highlight his point of view, however, he reaches two specious conclusions regarding my paper on indirect countertransference and candidate training requirements (JAPA 49/3). First, on page 194 he misreads me as separating the “reality” of training (indirect countertransference) from the fantasies it elicits for both candidate and analysand. Here he confuses a theoretical proposition with the realities of actual analytic work, thereby committing a category error. In theory it is possible to speak of indirect countertransference reactions as a separate reality, whereas in a control analysis they are part of, as Ehrlich notes, the “crucible in which the candidate's countertransferences and the analysand's transferences play themselves out.” The training requirements are an external reality and a trigger for indirect countertransference reactions in control cases, but the complexities of each unique candidate-analysand pair will shape the nature and extent of those reactions. My point was to highlight the need in theory to separate out the indirect countertransference reactions specific to analytic training, in order to facilitate the candidate's awareness of such triggers.

Second, Ehrlich says, “Jaffe (along with many other writers) implies that the intrusion of external factors is somehow unusual in candidate analyses.” While I cannot speak for other writers, I do want to make it clear that this writer holds no such belief. Various external factors can and will impact analytic work throughout the analyst's career. My point was merely to draw attention to some external factors relevant specifically to control analyses.

[[RunningHead]]

The Supervisory Alliance: Facilitating the Psychotherapist's Learning Experience. Edited By Susan Gill. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2002, xx + 242 pp., $45.00

Helen Gediman

Helen Gediman

55 East 87th Street #IB
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-831-5561
E-mail: Helengediman@aol.com

Helen Gediman   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, New York Freudian Society; Clinical Professor of Psychology, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

It is hard to imagine that not too long ago, the supervisory process was neither formally studied nor taught in most psychoanalytic institutes. Supervisors would rarely call for openly conveying what they transacted privately behind the closed doors of their consulting rooms. This compendium, in contrast, ably edited by Susan Gill, explicates the forthright teaching and learning of supervision and encourages systematic teaching of the psychoanalytic principles relevant to the supervisory process in psychoanalysis proper and in psychoanalytic therapies. It is a most welcome addition to a growing literature that reflects the continuous study of the supervisory process despite resistances and anxieties about exposing one's work in settings in which the institute has real power over the professional fates of supervisors and supervisees.

Supervision as a triadic system is central to our understanding of supervision (see Gediman and Wolkenfeld1980; Kantrowitz 2002) and is the major thematic thread throughout this book. Wolkenfeld and I initially proposed the idea of supervision as a triadic system in our attempts to explicate parallel process in psychoanalysis and supervision. Since then, however, analysts have studied interactions among various dyads of the supervisory triad in contexts that go significantly beyond an attempt to understand parallelisms as such. The resonance of conscious and unconscious wishes on the part of patient, analyst-supervisee, and supervisor (see Jacobs 2001)

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trauma

The Seductive Hypothesis

Steven Reisner

Steven Reisner

31 West 11th Street #6C
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: Sreisner@psychoanalysis.net

Steven Reisner   Adjunct Professor of Psychiatry, New York University School of Medicine; teaching faculty, New York University Psychoanalytic Institute and New York University International Trauma Studies Program. Submitted for publication May 19, 2002.

In much of contemporary culture, “trauma” signifies not so much terrible experience as a particular context for understanding and responding to a terrible experience. In therapy, in the media, and in international interventions, the traumatized are seen not simply as people who suffer and so are deserving of concern and aid; they are seen also as people who suffer for us, who are given special dispensation. They are treated with awe if they tell a certain kind of trauma story, and are ignored or vilified if they tell another. Trauma has become not simply a story of pain and its treatment, but a host of sub-stories involving the commodifi-cation of altruism, the justification of violence and revenge, the entry point into “true experience,” and the place where voyeurism and witnessing intersect. Trauma is today the stuff not only of suffering but of fantasy. Historically, trauma theory and treatment have shown a tension, exemplified in the writings of Freud and Janet, between those who view trauma as formative and those who view it as exceptional. The latter view, that trauma confers exceptional status deserving of special privilege, has gained ground in recent years and has helped to shape the way charitable dollars are distributed, how the traumatized are presented in the media, how governments justify and carry out international responses to trauma, and how therapists attend to their traumatized patients. This response to trauma reflects an underlying, unarticulated belief system derived from narcissism; indeed, trauma has increasingly become the venue, in society and in treatment, where narcissism is permitted to prevail.

[[RunningHead]]

Analysis and Psychotherapy by Telephone: Twenty years of Clinical Experience

Mark Leffert

Mark Leffert

2233 Anacapa Street
Santa Barbara, CA 93105
Fax: 805-898-1822
E-mail: Mleffert@aol.com

Mark Leffert   Training and Supervising Analyst, Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute. Submitted for publication March 5, 2001.

Psychoanalysis via telephone is becoming increasingly prevalent while remaining an area of comparatively little study. The author's early telephone treatment of a series of patients living some distance away or engaged in business travel, and his subsequent telephone treatment of nine analytic and five psychotherapy patients following his own geographic move, are discussed in detail. The mechanics of beginning and carrying out such treatment are examined. The theoretical implications of the shift to the telephone and the ambivalence with which it is often met by clinicians are also explored. The role of nonverbal communication in both in-person and telephone analysis is considered, as is the concept of the analytic office as a literal space and a psychological container. Suggestions for future research are advanced.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Forbidden Quest and The Slippery Slope

Roots of Authoritarianism in Psychoanalysis

Frederic Levine

Frederic Levine

7600 Red Road
Suite 108
Miami, FL 33143-5421
E-mail: Fredericjl@aol.com

Frederic Levine  

Until quite recently it was common for psychoanalysts in the United States, and for their organizations and training institutes, to express concern about a specter known as the “slippery slope.” According to this view, psychoanalytic theories and methods are constantly at risk of being corrupted or diluted (“going down the slippery slope toward … ”), because of unconscious resistances that are present in everyone. These forces work against recognizing and preserving the truths revealed by psychoanalysis, and they impede efforts to use its methods effectively in pursuit of those truths with patients.

Robert Waelder (1960) articulated the matter clearly: “In science, as in most other affairs, we should first try to keep what we already have—cultivate the land that has been cleared and guard it against a return of the jungle and against corrosion—and then try to improve on it or to enlarge it… Psychoanalysis—its intellectual substance and the categorical imperative of ceaseless self-exploration which provides its moral mainspring—is always in jeopardy because it arouses strong resistances from which nobody seems to be exempt …” (pp. x, xii; emphasis added).

To ward off the risk posed by these resistances—that is, for what were understood to be very good reasons—virtually every psychoanalytic organization and institute, and many individual analysts, took measures, consciously and unconsciously, to protect the discipline and its institutions through regulations and controls.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Florida Psychoanalytic Institute and New York Freudian Society.

The author is grateful to Michael P. Kowitt, Helen Banta, Jacob A. Arlow, and Deborah A. Levine for helpful comments on earlier drafts. Submitted for publication September 20, 2002.

[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Psychoanalytic Technique. By Otto Fenichel, translated by David Brunswick. New York: Psychoanalytic Quarterly Press, 1969, 130 pp. $39.99.

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02446
E-mail: Judy_Kantrowitz@hms.harvard.edu

Judy Kantrowitz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; Associate Professor of Psychology, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

Otto Fenichel's Problems of Psychoanalytic Technique, originally published in 1941 and again in 1969 in paper by the Psychoanalytic Quarterly Press, is at once conspicuously dated and strikingly modern in its exposition of psychoanalytic theory and technique.

All assumptions stem from a belief in the economic theory of drives and the primacy of infantile sexuality. Focus on the drives leads Fenichel to conceptualize the central analytic goal as the transformation of infantile sexuality into genital sexuality; achieving that goal is facilitated by the freeing of dammed-up sexual energy, which in turn strengthens the orgastic response. Most analysts today are not so wedded to the theory of drives, but even those for whom it remains central would likely view these ideas as overly simplistic and limited. Aggression as a drive has come to hold a primacy at least equal to the sexual drive, and for many years it has been dominant at least among analysts in the United States, where issues around authority have become prominent since the late 1960's. Only recently has attention returned to sexuality—and now, attachment would be viewed as equivalent in importance by many analysts.

Fenichel's conceptualization of the psychosexual stages as discrete has likewise been superseded. Analysts today have integrated those pioneering ideas about infantile sexuality with the rich array of more recent discoveries rooted in both clinical experience and observational data. Contemporary psychoanalytic developmental theory has evolved a much more complex picture of overlapping stages, colored and influenced by other factors, such as cognitive development. There is no longer a belief that character and conflict are fixed by the age of five or that the capacity for emotional and cognitive flexibility are frozen at a particular point in development.

313

Ronald Britton  

I am glad to have this opportunity to respond to Henry Smith's discussion; it is a pleasure to address such intelligent questions and perceptive comments. The thrust of many of his comments appears to be that it is the phenomenology of psychoanalysis that matters, and that theory should be “last in” and “first out” when we review our analytic findings. I wholeheartedly agree with that. But first we have to clarify when we are describing phenomena and when we are articulating theory, and this is not always easy. It also means we have to try to be clearer about our theories, and that I will try to do.

I would like to respond to Smith's question on the theory of belief I have put forward.

[[RunningHead]]

Ghosts of Paradigms past

The once and future evolution of psychoanalytic thought

Arnold Wilson

Arnold Wilson

80 University Place, #4A
New York, Ny I0003
E-mail: Dr.arnoldwilson@verizon.net

Arnold Wilson   Faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR).

An example of the psychoanalytic mode of thought is put forward concerning how psychoanalytic theories have historically been constituted and transformed. The model of world hypotheses, characterized by multiple irresolvable truth claims, captures the nature of most psychoanalytic theorizing until about 1970. Each of two world hypotheses—one grounded in intrapsychic conflict (seen when the analyst observes from outside the transference) and the other in interpersonal internalization (seen when the analyst observes from inside the bidirectional interactive processes)—is an autonomous and self-sufficient aggregate. The stance taken by the analyst-observer with respect to the analytic interaction is key to seeing how the two world hypotheses are made manifest in clinical work and in theory. By contrast, the model of competing programs captures the essential nature of most psychoanalytic theorizing since about 1970, and is characterized by the necessity of each progressively evolving through a particular kind of commerce with its neighbors. Such commerce is necessary when a program is in danger of degenerating. In this way of thinking, there is a fundamental tension between classical psychoanalysis adapting to the demands and exigencies of its particular and ever evolving historical niche and simultaneously retaining the core commitments that guarantee continuity. Honoring the forces of progression displaces the quest for truth as a paramount goal of psychoanalysis. A developmental lag in recognizing this transformation has hindered progress toward a comparative, process-centered psychoanalysis.

The Good Fight: Psychoanalysis in the Age of Managed Care

Robert Pyles

Robert Pyles

367 Worcester Road
Wellesley, MA 02481
Fax: 781-235-6310
E-mail: Rpylesmd@comcast.net

Robert Pyles  

At its midwinter meeting every second year, this association graciously grants its immediate past president the privilege of speaking to the membership about issues of special note or concern. It is my singular opportunity to be the first president to make such an address at the start of a millenium—an opportunity that mandates a look back to our founding in 1911, an assessment of where we have come since, and an exploration of where we must go in this new century.

We stand at a crossroads. The strength of our will and the clarity of our vision will determine our future. It is my great hope that in three to five years we can look back as a profession and see our members, our association, and our societies fully engaged in the major political and social issues of our time; an extensive national and local network of psychoanalysts interacting with the media; a lean, effective, and responsive governance in our national and local organizations; and a new spirit of experimentation and collaboration among our colleagues in the profession. To achieve these goals, we will need to understand the factors that underlie our highest ideals, and build on our inherent strengths to further our mission. It is in this context that I address myself to our history, to our culture, and to our future.

Psychoanalysis today is under siege. We are fighting for survival—the survival of our profession and of our ability to deliver quality care to our patients. It is a hard fight, one that in the beginning left us at times feeling helpless and despairing. In fact, when managed care, the Clinton plan, and draconian legislative measures such as the Maryland databank law crashed down upon us in 1992, we were helpless. At least,

Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East; past president, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Plenary Address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, November 25, 2000. Submitted for publication August 28, 2001.

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl   Faculty, Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Jacques Szaluta's Psychohistory: Theory and Practrice is a textbook, written in textbook style, and aspiring to cover the territory of its subject clearly and thoroughly. It opens with a Webster's model definition that sets the tone: “psychohistory is the application of psychology, in its broadest sense, of psychoanalysis in a specific sense, to the study of the past, of history.” So, to explore the field, we need to know about what is being applied, psychoanalysis, and about the ways in which it has been applied, and we need to know about that to which psychoanalysis has been applied, history, and the areas and types of history involved. Psychohistory covers this two-part agenda item by item.

On the psychoanalysis side, Szaluta offers a brief, workmanlike survey of Freud's main concepts and a look at the way in which Freud himself explored historical topics in works like Totem and Taboo and Civilization and Its Discontents. These pages may be of help to graduate students in history, but psychoanalysts will find them boring.

361

Peter Fonagy's new book begins with a statement of his interest and allegiance to the “two cultures of psychology”: psychoanalysis, which emphasizes meaning in its attempt to understand experience and therapeutic change, and academic experimental psychology, which emphasizes reliable, measurable observation. He acknowledges that “there is bad blood between psychoanalysis and attachment theory.” To counter this mutual suspicion, he advances the thesis that attachment theory has a foot in both cultures, the world of meaning and the world of measurement, and so is ideally suited to encourage and show the way to a rapprochement of psychoanalysis with experimental psychology. Although the book is addressed to members of both cultures, it provides far more background and preliminary explanation of attachment theory for psychoanalysts than vice versa.

[[RunningHead]]

Guilt About Being Born and Debt Concerning the Gift of Life

Andrew Lotterman

Andrew Lotterman

670 West End Avenue #IC
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: AndyL1978@aol.com

Andrew Lotterman   Faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons. Submitted for publication October 12, 2001.

A very powerful form of preoedipal guilt stems from a sense of having damaged the life-giving parent with one's most basic needs. A related phenomenon is the child's sense that another subjectivity has deliberately chosen to give and sustain the child's life. This experience gives rise to a compelling mixture of awe, reverence, and obligation, a sense of guilt about being born, of debt concerning the gift of life. This sense of guilt and debt are part of every child's development. Depending on how conflicts surrounding them are negotiated, the child can come to feel either an appetite for living or a crushing sense of selfishness and unworthiness. Such forms of guilt and debt may lead not only to masochism generally, but to a particularly pernicious form of negative therapeutic reaction that can sabotage psychoanalytic work. A patient may feel, for instance, that a successful psychoanalysis is a selfish indulgence and an immoral betrayal of a sacred duty to rescue his or her parents from their misery. Guilt about being born and the sense of debt concerning the gift of life are linked to various developmental, psychodynamic, interpersonal, and cultural phenomena.

[[RunningHead]]

A Brief History of Psychoanalysis in Argentina

Samuel Arbiser

Samuel Arbiser

Luis Agote 2437, 2°
(1425) Ciudad de Buenos Aires
ARGENTINA
Fax: 54-11-4803-8678
E-mail: Samiar@fibertel.com.ar

Samuel Arbiser  

The peculiar development—in both extension and depth—of psychoanalysis and its practice in Argentina, has been the object of interest and curiosity in diverse spheres. Inquiry into the determining factors of the phenomenal expansion, in this corner of the planet, of an avant-garde discipline requiring such sophisticated practice is an ongoing project. This article is necessarily an informal chronicle that may contribute, if incidentally and modestly, to answering questions better addressed by historians and social scientists.

Argentina is quite different from most other Latin American countries. A large portion of its population had its origins in European immigration, and as a result of the struggles to organize the country as a nation, the porteña1 current associated with European cultural values and ideals of progress prevailed in the country from the second half of the nineteenth century. During that period, the ruling classes that governed the nation were imbued with liberal democratic intellectual ideals. For instance, a law making secular education universal and obligatory has been in force since the 1880s. Moreover, the country enjoyed an enviable prosperity from the last decades of the nineteenth century up to the 1930 world economic crisis. Buenos Aires was therefore a natural site for the introduction of avant-garde ideas being developed in Europe.

The history of psychoanalysis in Argentina may be divided, perhaps a bit arbitrarily, into five periods: (1) a preinstitutional period, (2) a pioneering period, (3) a period of consolidation, (4) a period that comprises the crisis of the 1970s, and (5) the present.

1Belonging to the port—that is, to the city of Buenos Aires.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Buenos Aires Psychoanalytic Association.

The editors thank Irene Cairo for her thoughtful assistance in preparing this article for publication.

The first edition of The Technique of Psychoanalysis was first published in 1928. It consisted of a verbatim report of six lectures delivered as a “training course” at the London Institute of Psychoanalysis in the days before systematic instruction in psychoanalytical technique was initiated; the series was based on a relatively brief experience of psychoanalytic practice. At that time, the literature on psychoanalytic technique was non-existent, and considerable uncertainty existed among practicing analysts in England regarding many points of procedure. Accordingly, it seemed desirable to integrate the theoretical and practical aspects of the “analytical situation” in one presentation.

When Glover revised his first edition of this book, he wondered whether concepts from 1928 would still be relevant in 1955.

318

Psychology Of The Sopranos: Love, Death, Desire and Betrayal in America's Favorite Gangster Family. By Glen O. Gabbard. New York: Basic Books, 2002, 224 pp., $22.00.

Leon Levin

Leon Levin

E-mail: Lalevin@erols.com

Leon Levin   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Maryland.

In his preface, Glen O. Gabbard writes that he is taking “a hard, but lighthearted look” at the existential dilemmas of love, death, desire, and betrayal as they are showcased in the TV drama, The Sopranos. The drama centers about a New Jersey mob boss, Tony Soprano, whose panic attacks drive him to the office of psychoanalyst Dr. Jennifer Melfi. Gabbard, who engages zestfully with popular culture without condescension, understands that a good teacher is one who can be taught. He sees himself as exploring what the drama teaches us about psychology and psychotherapy. Its creators are almost all gifted writers and actors who have had extensive personal experience with psychoanalysis and psychotherapy, and he respects them as serious students of human nature.

Psychoanalysts will appreciate the reflections of a wise, perceptive clinician on the conflicts of the characters. Tony is seen as torn by the tension between his divergent selves. He is a brutal thug and a faithless husband, but at the same time he loves his wife and hopes for his children to live the American dream. He splits his contradictory selves to avoid anxiety.

Gabbard points out that this type of splitting is common among charismatic leaders and that some degree of splitting of incompatible selves is present in all of us. Tony tries to hide his criminal self from his children, but he is doomed to fail because the necessary degree of deception is unsustainable. Although Tony is antisocial he is not considered a psychopath because he suffers. In his initial dream of birds flying away from his pool, he reveals his fear of loss of love and abandonment; this is convincingly interpreted by Dr. Melfi. In some sense Tony is Everyman, at the same time tragic and comic.

365
