Nancy Goodman   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society, Washington, DC, Program.

At the Air and Space Museum in Washington, DC, an exhibit takes the viewer on a journey from the skin of a person into the molecular structure of DNA, followed by a shift telescoping out into the solar system and galaxies surrounding the individual. The ten chapters in this collection, the second in a series on primitive states, take readers on a

674

similar journey: first into the detailed activity of affect communication and then into the broader world of biology, neurology, genetics, affect regulation, archeology, and chaos theory. The book' dust jacket shows a thriving toddler atop a depiction of the double helix. While biology is present in the book, for these authors it is the explanatory power of projective identification that is considered the DNA of all communication. Analysts and therapists with other theories of mind may find the ubiquitous presence of this idea a challenge to their reading and yet welcome the fund of knowledge and clinical acuity presented in these chapters.

The authors in this volume are associated with psychoanalytic institutes in California, Argentina, and Spain and find their psychoanalytic home in the ideas of Bion and Klein; they are aware that their ideas are new to many North American readers. It is their explication of recent work on projective identification, along with their integrative approach and the beauty of their clinical descriptions, that will attract readers wanting to understand better how Bionian analysts think and work. James Grotstein' chapter is particularly informative and helpful for the newcomer. He presents an historical guide to the development of the concept of projective identification and its utility in understanding infant development.

Allan Schore, who has written a remarkably comprehensive book on human development, Affect Regulation and the Origin of the Self: The Neurobiology of Emotional Development (1994), uses his expertise in psychoanalysis and neurobiology to focus here on the role of projective identification and affect communication. His chapter clearly and thoroughly brings together diverse areas of research, weaving together information about early infant interaction, nonverbal communication, affect facial recognition, and (most important) the central role of the right brain for affect regulation and communication. Schore uses the latest knowledge gained through the new technologies of neurological research to address questions about how inner experience is laid down and made available in and out of analytic sessions. Schore places projective identification as the central mechanism though which empathy and attachment take place, whether between infant and mother, right brain and left brain, or analysand and analyst.

Kay McKenzie

Kay McKenzie

1611 South Green Road, Suite 302
Cleveland, OH 44121

Kay McKenzie   Faculty, Cleveland Psychoanalytic Center; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University Hospitals, Cleveland, OH.

Most therapists of psychotic patients develop a “psychotic feel”; it can be a sense of mental pressure, confusion, or dullness. The psychotic feel is quite personal, but is consistent within each therapist. Piera Aulagnier, who first published this book in French in 1975, when confronted with psychotic discourse, went beyond the feeling and tried to understand the meaning of the psychotic communication. She reports that she

681

often felt that she was receiving it “as the wild interpretation made to the analyst of the non-evidence of the evident” (p. xxv). She found that the theory bequeathed us by Freud did not address all of what she experienced when trying to analyze psychotic patients. She hoped that by expanding the theory to account for what is elaborated in psychosis, her analytic work with psychotic patients would become more productive. She observed how these patients confidently oppose their delusion to the reality of the analyst. As there is no doubt about reality for either analyst or patient, she asks if there might not have been a time for each when the psyche encountered a discourse that could not be doubted, a discourse in which the communication of the object formed the representation of the self and the understanding of the world.

Aulagnier sees psychic development as balanced between drives, the need for satisfaction, and thought, the need to “make sense.” In working with psychotic patients, she found that communications from the mother (or the parents), both in words and in action, had led either to painful answers to essential questions concerning how the child came to have life or to secrets that could not be spoken or thought. The problematic questions in psychosis are questions of origin: Did you desire me? How did I come to be? She describes a pattern in which the mothers of future psychotic patients did not want a child, or did not want this child. The mother imposes on the child, to an excessive degree, her own idea of how the child is to behave and think. The child is not just to eat, but to eat “properly”; the child is not to sleep, but to sleep “properly.” The child experiences this psychic intrusion as violence; there may be actual physical violence as well. Thinking becomes confounded, and the continuity of thought is shredded. It is necessary for the child to make sense of the discontinuity and pain of this experience, which can be done only by arriving at a distorted representation of self, of the parents, and of society. Aulagnier considers this “primary delusional thinking” a necessary but insufficient condition for the later development of psychosis.

Kenneth King

Kenneth King

1687 114th Avenue SE, Suite 125

Kenneth King   Training and Supervising Analyst, and Supervising Child/Adolescent Analyst, Seattle Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Associate Professor, Deptartment of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, University of Washington.

Between 1994 and 1995 the New York Freudian Society asked a series of speakers to address the issue of omnipotence; this book is a compilation of most of the papers that resulted. As with most such collections, the quality and originality of the papers vary, but there is no question as to the breadth of the viewpoints presented.

689

Richard Almond leads off with a clinically useful review of the concept of omnipotence and its transferential manifestations. Almond stresses that it is the attempt to use power that can be observed interactively, whereas omnipotent fantasies involve a subjective state that is inferred by the observer. He reviews the contributions of Freud, Adler, Winnicott, Robert White, and Kohut, noting that “while omnipotence plays a limited role in Freud's metapsychology, it is carried to the other extreme in Adler's and Kohut's theories, where, through the omnipotence implicit in the striving for power or the need for the functions of the selfobject, it is everywhere. Winnicott is somewhere between these two …”(p. 10).

The heart of Almond's contribution is his clinical illustrations of omnipotent thinking through control over the other, control over the self, timelessness, omnipotence through thought, masochism, and drivenness. He closes with a review of development, positing that the sense of power begins very early in life and continues to be elaborated throughout the life cycle.

The paper by Jack and Kerry Kelly Novick stands in stark contrast to the others here. The Novicks continue their focus on sadomasochism as an integral aspect of all psychopathology and point to omnipotent functioning as the sign of a sadomasochistic solution to conflict. They do not find omnipotence to play a role in normal development; rather, its presence is the result of real failures, particularly in parental care. They distinguish and describe in rich detail the development of two systems of self-esteem regulation, the omnipotent and the competent, along the way taking issue with the classical views that the anal phase necessarily involves sadism and that normal adolescence is always turbulent. Their extensive exploration of the evolution of omnipotence across developmental phases is a real strength of this paper. Finally, the Novicks conclude with recommendations for clinical technique. Their all too brief description of facilitating the development of the therapeutic alliance and monitoring changes in it as it matures during the course of the analysis is itself a worthy contribution to the clinical literature.

Ephi Betan

Ephi Betan   Assistant Professor and Director of Practicum Training, Georgia School of Professional Psychology; advanced candidate, Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute.

The editors of this volume begin with an anecdote: in 1909, Freud responded to Jung's enthusiasm about introducing psychoanalysis in America, saying, “Little do they know we are bringing the plague.” Postmodernism, they suggest, may be the second plague. It is a curious metaphor attributed to Freud and borrowed by the current editors to refer to a self-described revolution. Plague conjures up destruction (locusts), disease (bubonic plague), nuisance or suffering (“to be plagued with worry”). The editors note they chose this phrase to capture “a backlash, hostile responses” coming from psychoanalysts outside of postmodern, relational theoretical circles. I venture to say the use of this metaphor is multidetermined and also reflects struggles within, not only without, the postmodern psychoanalytic movement. Just as some believe that the first session contains the story of an analysis, perhaps the title speaks to the pulls and sways of the text and its authors. Indeed, as I proceeded to the last of the series of articles (some new, some previously published) by key players in the postmodern psychoanalytic movement, I became ever more convinced that the title encapsulated the authors' struggles with their allegiance to postmodern perspectives. The use of a metaphor of destruction to describe a movement that is expanding how we think about psychoanalytic theory and practice speaks to an ambivalence that occurs when passion and aggression emerge in tandem. Aggression, in its nonpejorative sense, exists in any effort toward change and growth. These authors are committed to psychoanalysis and committed also to pushing the discipline further. It is a delicate task to preserve a rich discipline while bringing into relief its tensions, contradictions, and extremes.

To the credit of the authors, this volume poses many complex questions and engages a spectrum of postmodern and psychoanalytic topics: philosophy, sociopolitics, truth, objectivity, meaning, normality/abnormality, health/pathology, sex, sexuality, perversion, self-mutilation, gender, race, ethnicity, trauma, self, pluralism, subjectivity, and intersubjectivity. The list is incomplete, for these are only the ideas I can capture in a word. This is a dense and sophisticated text with more ideas than I could do justice to in this review.

Janice Lieberman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Hans Bellmer (1902-1975) was a Polish-born surrealist artist whose best-known works were made in Berlin during the Nazis' ascendancy (1933-4). In 1938 he moved to France, where he remained for the rest of his life. Bellmer considered his works to be a conscious act of defiance against German fascism and his father. He created and photographed two life-size pubescent dolls, which he distorted, dismembered, or menaced in sinister scenarios that sometimes included himself. These scenarios seemed to be nightmarish manifestations of a journey into his unconscious. Sue Taylor says in this psychobiog-raphy that “Bellmer depicted female bodies reified, mutated, sodomized, bound, eviscerated” (p. 2). The “degenerative” bodies of the dolls were a challenge to the cult of “the perfect body” prevalent in Germany. Another Bellmer scholar, Alain Jouffroy (1999), has described the dolls quite differently; he considers them manifestations of the “physical unconscious,” as opposed to Freud's “mental unconscious.” Bellmer himself professed that he was attempting with his dolls to show how it felt to be inside the female body, and that the distortions represented the inner body ego as seen from the outside.

Hans Bellmer: The Anatomy of Anxiety is a comprehensive and scholarly book based on the author's doctoral dissertation. Sue Taylor is assistant professor of art history at Portland State University, and an art critic and curator as well. She consulted for this book with John and Mary Gedo, and cites in her text the psychoanalytic writings of Freud, Bak, Chasseguet-Smirgel, Gilman, Greenacre, Kris, and Kuspit, among others. Her aim, she says, is “to set the record straight about Bellmer,” and she takes on the daunting task of examining the gruesome dolls and photographs as well as his later paintings and prints. Previous studies of Bellmer focused on the early works; she painstakingly examines Bellmer's entire oeuvre as well as his many writings, autobiographical and otherwise. Taylor leads the reader into the dark recesses of unconscious thought and fantasy as she checks out her hypotheses against Bellmer's works. Fetishism, sadomasochism, and anal eroticism prevail. Taylor considers the works to be more pornographic than erotic, and not just sadistic and mysogynistic, as others have stated, but also (because of his identification with women) masochistic as well.

Bruce Reis  

The American invited ambassadors of the relational perspective—who challenge accepted models of thought and action in psychoanalysis and in the larger world as a matter of intellectual self-definition and are, accordingly, barred from membership in the host organization—to share their views in an all-day presentation. Steven Cooper, the chair of the proceedings, began by paying tribute to the late Stephen Mitchell, whose development of relational theory directly questioned the authority of psychoanalytic cannon. Recalling Mitchell's passion for a two-person model of analysis, Cooper indicated that each of the panelists would continue to ask the difficult questions that had become a hallmark of the relational reexamination of time-honored analytic concepts.

The first speaker was Lewis Aron, who sought to clarify some prevailing misconceptions regarding relational theory. He identified the relational trend in psychoanalysis as “more a sensibility than a school,” and, while acknowledging Mitchell's seminal contributions, explained that relational theory did not develop exclusively around a single leader as previous analytic schools (Freudian, Kleinian, and Winnicottian) did, but rather thrived on pluralistic voices, sometimes at odds with each other (see Mitchell and Aron 1999). Tension, ambiguity, and paradox are key concepts for relationalists, Aron went on, defining them as dynamic, shifting forces moving forward a conversation that is not bent on seeking resolution or consensus.

Addressing the putative fundamentalism (Greenberg 2001) and heresies (Busch 2001)

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 22, 2001. Panelists: Steven Cooper (chair), Lewis Aron, Ken Corbett, Jody Messler Davies, Adrienne Harris, Irwin Hoffman.

The witch-of-us? Complex and the Wizard of OZ

Jeffrey Stern

Jeffrey Stern

767 North Michigan Avenue (# 3607)
Chicago, IL 60611
Fax: 312-787-2208
E-mail: Sternjeffrey@earthlink.net>

Jeffrey Stern   Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Lecturer in Psychiatry, University of Chicago and Rush University. Submitted for publication September 30, 2002.

Works of applied psychoanalysis normally use psychoanalytic theory to reveal the secret meanings of works of art. An attempt is made to reverse the directionality of such analyses and see whether a work of art, The Wizard of Oz, has something to teach psychoanalysis about adolescent female psychosexual development. The author argues that the popularity and importance of the film is an effect of its symbolic representation of a girl's entry into menarche, and the meaning of this milestone for herself and for her mother. He addresses ideas about feminine castration fears (or what more recently have been called fears of genital injury), issues about menopause, and fantasies—both surprising and violent—around the meanings of menarche for both mother and daughter.

Identity Maintenance in the Affectively Distant Patient

Irwin Hoffman

Irwin Hoffman

1240 Westlake Blvd.
Suite 211
Westlake Village, CA 91361
Fax: 805-494-0157

Irwin Hoffman   Member, Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies. Submitted for publication April 5, 2002.

Patients who are affectively distant, in that they appear to have little conscious emotional investment in the analyst, have been described increasingly in the psychoanalytic literature of the last twenty years. Typically, they have been understood either from a developmental point of view as defensively struggling against wishes for symbiotic union or, on the Kleinian model, as having unconscious fantasies of bodily fusion with the mother that, upon separation from her, result in annihilation anxiety that generates autistic defenses. Of special importance is the work of Heinz Lichtenstein, who stresses early identity maintenance and the role of mirroring experiences with the mother in the development of an “identity theme.” This concept is used here as a symbiotic precursor of ego identity that ties the self to a particular mother. It is this primitive form of identity that can occasion regressive self-definition in the transference of the affectively distant patient. Two cases are presented that illustrate dynamics and transference dispositions occurring in the psychoanalysis of these patients. The discussion focuses on the role of the patient's catastrophic fear of acceptance, as well as on the consequent need for self-protective measures. It is argued that careful and consistent analysis of these conflicted areas of these patients' transference leads toward greater integration of their identity and personality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anna Aragno Responds to Review

Anna Aragno

Anna Aragno

140 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10023

Anna Aragno  

August 3, 2002. I read with regret Mary Target's review of my book Symbolization: Proposing a Developmental Paradigm for a New Psychoanalytic Theory of Mind (JAPA, 50/1), less for its tone than for its failure to identify or grasp many of the reconceptualizations that are the heart of the book's content and purpose.

Rather than address the revisions that are proposed, the reviewer resorts to dismissives, correctives, and judgments, meted out with an authoritative grandeur that leaves little room for debate. The acidic aftertaste left on the palate of the “innocent reader” of such a review is due to the subtle, yet unmistakable, flavor of “seniority” with which it is imbibed. Perhaps this is due to the fact that the book was derived from my dissertation, a point the reviewer considers worth mentioning.

Unfortunately, in her fervor to rush in and criticize my unorthodox “style” and poorly structured sentences, Target fails to look carefully enough beyond their imperfect form to see the conceptual complexities they actually refer to. This pervasive interpretive discrepancy between what is written and how it is read, a classic symptom of “epistemological dissonance,” produces an alarmingly distorted picture of the book's content. I believe both derisions and distortions to be due to a total absence of a joint, or compatible, conceptual and referential framework, and hence to a genuine failure in understanding what the revision is all about. This is precisely what Kuhn alerted us to, what I expected, and what indeed occurs in the midst of a paradigm shift: the entrenchment of the reviewer in the conceptual mold of an old world-view beautifully illustrates what happens when different paradigms meet. Epistemologically, philosophically, and methodologically, Target and I live on different planets! And, as Saint-Exupery so poetically noted, different planets go by different laws. The very nature of the principles underlying this paradigm shift are “invisible” to those on Target's planet! We may be looking at the same things, but we see them entirely differently.

Dale Boesky   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute.

J. B. Pontalis, who achieved fame for his collaboration with Jean Laplanche in The Language of Psychoanalysis, now reviews with subtle irony his life's efforts to define concepts in psychoanalysis. He is a master of paradox and nuance; he is by turns witty and profound; and in the end, I wished very much to have known him in person.

It becomes gradually apparent as one reads this book that Pontalis has discovered a unique and persuasive manner of describing the experience of being an analyst, and it is well worth thinking about how he manages to win the reader's confidence that he is indeed a master clinician. Partly this is the effect of his obvious passion for psychoanalysis, his equally obvious and genuine concern for the welfare of his patients, and his relentless skepticism about the reductive simplification of barren “concepts.” For example: “An interpretation that comes from what I know and not from what strikes me is not an interpretation. [It is] a deafness, a blindness, brought about by knowledge” (p. 96). He is an aphorist in the grand French tradition: “Forgetting is necessary to give thickness to time” (p. 69), and “Betray nothing of yourself, as if you weren't disclosing something in concealing yourself” (p. 50).

He is an enemy of the cliché and a champion of the unique immediacy of clinical experience. And he is not ashamed to say why he reveres the work of Sigmund Freud. This volume distills the wise experience of a gifted analyst, and will richly reward careful reading throughout the psychoanalytic community.

1383

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thoughts on authority and leadership

Richard Fox

Richard Fox

226 Monarch Bay
Dana Point, CA 92629
Fax: 714-499-5195
E-mail: Rpfoxmd@aol.com

Richard Fox  

Over the years, I have served in various roles in the American Psychoanalytic Association and so have experience with its many internal conflicts. I would like to review this experience and to explore some of the complexities of authority relationships and their impact on the governance of the association. The history of APsaA over the past half-century provides an interesting case study through which we can explore the dynamics of authority and leadership and examine how these forces have shaped that history. Almost since its reorganization some fifty years ago, APsaA has been the scene of almost continuous debates about the distribution of authority within it. These controversies have taken the form of disputes about certification and training analyst status and ostensibly polarize the group as “liberals” and “conservatives,” or training analysts and non–training analysts.

Psychoanalysts have extensive experience working in relationships in which process, communication, and the building of an alliance are essential. They cultivate their capacities for empathy and for maintaining a nonjudgmental attitude. Yet, in the internal workings of analytic organizations, these talents and capabilities are seldom apparent. It is striking that although authority and leadership involve individual and group dynamics played out in arenas with significant social and personal impact, they remain relatively ignored subjects in the psychoanalytic literature.

In my pursuit of this inquiry, theory followed practice, and I had to familiarize myself with concepts not native to psychoanalytic thinking. I will ask your indulgence as I present some analytically unfamiliar concepts in order to lend perspective to the more compelling “clinical facts” of APsaA's political history.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; past president, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Plenary Address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New Orleans, May 4, 2001. Submitted for publication January 29, 2003.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on "Psychoanalytic Discourse at the Turn of Our Century: A Plea for a Measure of Humility"

Owen Renik

Owen Renik

388 Market Street
Suite 1010
San Francisco, CA 94111
E-mail: Odrenik@aol.com

Owen Renik  

Can't we all just get along?

Certainly we can all agree with Arnold Richards that arrogance and sectarianism have drastically limited the possibilities for constructive discourse throughout the history of psychoanalysis. Certainly we can all agree, as well, that this is a most unfortunate situation, which could be remedied if each of us would only be more open-minded and respectful toward colleagues who propose ideas different from our own. Finally, I think that we can all agree that his plea for a much-needed humility, together with our endorsements of his plea, is unlikely to have any impact on the problem.

For as long as there has been parochialism in our field—i.e., since the beginning—analysts have been deploring parochialism. The difficulty is, of course, that one invariably feels oneself to be reasonable and receptive; it's always the other guy who is attacking a straw man. For example, Arnold Richards criticizes Stuart Pizer for caricaturing the traditional psychoanalyst. While I can see how the objection arises, I could just as easily criticize Richards for caricaturing Pizer's position: I believe that Pizer understands very well how many traditional analysts, in practice, ameliorate the stance prescribed by their theory. But their practice contradicts their theory, which has not yet been correspondingly revised; and this is the difficulty he is attempting to address.

The plea for a measure of humility is laudable, but I don't think it will do any good, because it doesn't address the fundamental problem.

119

Transference: Shibboleth or Albatross? By Joseph Schachter. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2002, 267 pp., $45.00.

David Wolitzky

David Wolitzky

New York, NY 10012
E-mail: Dwolitzky@aol.com

David Wolitzky   Director, Director NYU Psychology Clinic; faculty and Supervisor, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Over the course of his fifty-year career, Joseph Schachter has gradually abandoned the traditional Freudian conception of transference and its technical implications. He rejects the idea that what makes psychoanalysis distinctive is genetic transference interpretation in the context of analytic neutrality (Kernberg 1999) and asserts that a necessary and sufficient criterion of a psychoanalytic approach is that it recognizes the “power of unconscious forces” (p. 174). As implied in the subtitle of his book, the Freudian view of transference, the shibboleth of psychoanalysis, has in fact become an albatross. Yet it is hard to relinquish, according to Schachter, because it would threaten the identity of Freudian analysts, who already suffer from a great deal of uncertainty and from limited therapeutic effectiveness. The claim of limited benefit, however, is based on a selective citation of the literature (see, e.g., Sandell 2001, 2002).

In place of the traditional view, Schachter has come to embrace Gill's emphasis on the “here-and-now” transference. Schachter also endorses the trends that he regards as compatible with his emphasis on the “here-and-now” transference: a two-person psychology; herme-neutics; a more spontaneous responsiveness to the patient, with its allegedly less rule-bound approach to technique; an appreciation of psychoanalysis as an art; and an openness to the idea of posttermi-nation relationships.

Schachter's view implies that contemporary Freudian analysts devote the better part (Schachter would say the worst part)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analyzing the Traumatic Impact of Childhood Visual Impairment

Richard Hertel

Richard Hertel

505 East Huron Street (#305)
Ann arbor, MI 48104
Fax: 734-665-7131
Email: Rkhertel@umich.edu

Richard Hertel   Lecturer, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Professor of Psychology, University of Michigan Medical School.

Material is presented from three analyses involving the impact on adult functioning of childhood trauma to the visual system. The clinical process by which unrecognized but ongoing traumatic reactions are identified for subsequent analysis is described. It is of particular importance, with these patients, to recognize their lifelong experience of visual confusion resulting from problems in their visual anatomy. Only then can fantasies and affects related to these physically induced states of confusion be worked through. Working through these reactions promotes the neutralization of related primitive narcissistic affects, mourning, and realistic accommodation, and leads to a more intact and integrated sense of self, and a marked increase in self-esteem. With this newly integrated sense of self, the complex interaction between physically induced confusion states and more typical developmental conflicts with objects can be worked through, resulting in more realistic and intense cathexes of the external world. The trauma suffered by these patients had been greatly compounded by its having gone unrecognized. To miss this yet again in an analysis is to repeat the past and retraumatize the patient. These findings have clear implications for patients with other biologically related symptoms (e.g., ADD, ADHD, and dyslexia).

In Others' Eyes: An Analysis of shame. By Günter Harry Seidler. Translated by Andrew Jenkins. Madison, ct: International Universities Press, 2000, 383 pp. $52.00.

Charles Spezzano

Charles Spezzano

2252 Fillmore Street
San francisco, CA 94115
E-mail: Cjspezzano@aol.com

Charles Spezzano   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

As I made my way into the heart of this book, I recalled hearing a Samuel Beckett film described as being about a perceiving eye. The perceived and the perceiver are two aspects of each person. The perceiver is desperate to perceive and the perceived tries desperately to hide. Eventually, one wins. WithIn Others' Eyes: An Analysis of Shame, Günter Harry Seidler takes us inside the human struggle to tolerate what is “other/strange/unfamiliar” in our perception of others and, even more so, in our fantasies of their perceptions of us, where we suffer the shameful possibility of their being taken aback by our otherness, strangeness, and unfamiliarity.

Shame has traveled a long way, conceptually, from a not-internalized version of guilt or a defense against voyeuristic and exhibitionistic impulses, to the multitextured form and central place it has in Seidler's work. Alexander (1938) put forward a theory of a shame-guilt cycle in which aggression leads to guilt, which leads to inhibition of aggression, which occasions an experience of oneself as passive, which leads to shame and then to compensatory aggression and finally more guilt. Piers and Singer (1953) argued that shame is an internalization of the threat of loss of the ego ideal's love, while guilt is an internalization of the castrating father. Lynd (1958)

Furnishing the Mind: Concepts and their Perceptual Basis. By Jesse Prinz. Cambridge: MIT Press, 2002, 368 pp., $42.00.

The Mind Doesn't Work that way: The Scope and Limit of Computational Psychology. By Jerry Fodor. Cambridge: MIT Press, 2001, 144 pp., $14.95 paperback, $25.00 hardcover.

These books deal with the general topic of thinking and representation, both conscious and unconscious, a topic crucial to our ideas about psychoanalytic theory and a topic on which we have some very definite opinions. These opinions have become convictions that are so much a part of psychoanalytic training, and therefore of our own way of thinking, that it is difficult to consider them erroneous or even to contemplate other ideas of the subject. The central concept to be examined here is that of psychic representation, which in our field has had a range of definitions, from “a more or less consistent reproduction within the mind of a perception of a meaningful thing or object” (Moore and Fine 1990, p. 166) to “a network of neurons activated in tandem….

July 20, 2003. My suggestion to Alan Bass about the vast literature dealing with his “problem” was that it had been solved elsewhere. He now informs me that I understand neither the problem nor the solution. Unfortunately, I find his letter no more clarifying than his book. The debt that Bass claims I owe the reader is really his. I cannot hope to do more than refer Bass to a dictionary to look up processive, as in processive reality, and to see it defined as “moving forward”; to an article by Biswas—“The Symbolic and Concrete” (Int. J. Psycho-Anal.84:733-753)—to see how most scholars use “concrete”; and to just about anyone else for an explanation of irony.

Commentary on "Psychoanalytic Discourse at the Turn of Our Century: A Plea for a Measure of Humility"

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

Box 140
525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-746-5944
E-mail: Tshapiro@med.cornell.edu

Theodore Shapiro  

New Presbyter is but Old Priest writ Large.

—John Milton

Who among us can reject a call for humility, except for those surviving Nietzscheans who admit to no virtue in that value? It is unseemly to argue for pride and rectitude and not support the openness and dialogue espoused by Arnold Richards. At the same time, humility does not seem to me the right designation for the capacity to listen as a precursor to a true interchange of ideas. The arguments presented, especially that there is “an interplay between the political history of psychoanalysis and the nature of psychoanalytic discourse,” require that we examine the recent history of ideas about psychoanalysis as recorded by Richards. In so doing, we may be able to see the ideas as separate from the politics. He admits that the hegemony and tyrannical exclusion described may be the defensive armor of any camp threatened by new or deviant ideas and that each form of discourse may constrain creative exploration of new paths for psychoanalysis (“New Presbyter is but Old Priest writ Large”). Indeed, Richards's frequent use of the word dialectic reveals his belief and hope that synthesis is always better than thesis or antithesis. He seems to believe also that a strong sense of democracy in an organization is the means to open intellectual exchange.

114

E-mail: Fdbaud@cs.com

Francis Baudry  

May 25, 2003. I am delighted to have an opportunity to respond to Françoise Meltzer's thoughtful critique of my paper on Flaubert. Reading her letter I am aware of the gaps and misunderstandings that can separate two disciplines struggling to cooperate with each other and reach a common language instead of developing an adversarial relationship. I have experienced the struggle firsthand in my participation for seventeen years in the study group on psychoanalysis and literature of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

[[RunningHead]]

The Contemporary Psychoanalyst at Work: Education and Training †

Daniel Jacobs

Daniel Jacobs

Daniel Jacobs  

The papers by Caligor, Barron, and Ehrlich reflect a deepening trend in psychoanalytic education—namely, the attempt to understand in greater depth and with increasing precision the educational experiences of candidates. Illustrating the variety of ways in which those educational experiences can be explored, the papers may be seen as forming three concentric rings. Each essay occupies its own sphere yet is related to the others in the common attempt to make clearer some particular aspect of analytic education.

As the subtitle indicates, Reason and Passion is a tribute to the long and productive professional life of Hanna Segal. An articulate spokesperson and creative leader of the contemporary Kleinians, Segal is feted here for her contributions to analytic theory and practice by a group of leading psychoanalysts influenced and inspired by her work. (Her contributions to applied analysis are celebrated in a companion volume referred to, but regrettably not named, in the editor's introduction.)

I found Bell's Introductory Essay to be among the most useful and satisfying chapters in the book. A brief biographical sketch highlights the political and intellectual passion and commitment that marked Segal's formative years and has continued throughout her life.

[[RunningHead]]

The Development of Anorexia Nervosa: The Hunger Artists. by Sylvia Brody. Madison, CT: Internationl Universities Press, 2002, 255 pp., $33.50.

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

59 Sixth Avenue
San Francisco, CA 94118
E-mail: Nmoshe@earthlink.net

Nathan Szajnberg   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UC-San Francisco.

Our métier as psychoanalysts is to read lives backward. In this book, we have a rich opportunity to read lives forward, and see ailments develop.

Sylvia Brody has written a book about two children, now young women, followed from birth as part of a study of “normal” volunteers. This study has produced four books, including the present one, following the girls from birth to the age of eighteen. The developmental study began in 1964 with 131 mothers and infants and was designed to determine the impact of maternal feeding on development. Brody developed observable criteria for maternal caregiving, clustered into categories of empathy, efficiency, and competence. She also examined the relative importance of feeding, holding, and proximity in infants' development. What began as a one-year study was re-funded for follow-ups at three, four, five, six, and seven years, and then eighteen, with developmental assessments and home and school visits. Belatedly, some fathers were included. Unfortunately, Brody's early methodology preceded Ainsworth's maternal sensitivity and attachment measures, which would have lent greater robustness to the results. Nevertheless, we are grateful for the detailed observations of these two girls who in adolescence developed eating disorders.

Thomä (1967) followed anorexics for several decades following the onset of overt symptoms. Phenomenologically, they did poorly, although the symptoms varied. Some remained eating-disordered (some developing obesity), while others developed personality disorders or fell into substance abuse—conditions with grim prospects under earlier treatment regimes. We must learn more about the early factors leading to eating disorders, a Moloch that consumes its subjects, that we may treat these women more effectively and liberate them.

Brody turns the telescope backward in time, showing us what infancy and childhood were like before the eruption of eating disorder symptoms at puberty. Read this book for the following details about the two young girls: (1) in infancy, frequent, medically unexplained vomiting—one child shoved her fist or various objects into her mouth to elicit vomiting (as described also in Winnicott 1965); (2) prolonged, inconsolable screaming fits.

Life Drive and Death Drive, Libido and Lethe: A Formalized Consistent Model of Psychoanalytic Drive and Structure Theory. By Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau; translated by Philip Slotkin. New York: Other Press, 2001, xi + 312 pp., $50.00.

William Greenstadt

William Greenstadt

New York, NY 10016
232 Madison Avenue
Fax: 212-686-4104
E-mail: W.greenstadt@worldnet. att.net

William Greenstadt   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society and the National Psychological Association for Psychoanalysis.

Interest in the “general theory” of psychoanalysis seems to be revived periodically, despite the many criticisms and rejections to which it has been subjected. Perhaps it reappears, Phoenix-like, as each new generation of psychoanalysts takes the field and seeks to reassure itself of its affiliation with the spirit of science. This book is yet another attempt to reformulate Freud's metapsychology, this time with great rigor and precision.

Is psychoanalytic metapsychology a system of explanation of mentalistically described facts based on known and inferred characteristics of the nervous system? Is the solution of the mind-brain dilemma actually achieved, or at least achievable, by a dynamically conceived psychology?

Metapsychology is viewed by Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau as the intermediate or linking theory between the clinical facts of psychoanalysis and the “close-to-the-data” theories that arise from them, on the one hand, and, on the other, the emerging theories of brain function. Clinical facts, she says, are experiential observations that are essentially descriptive, while theory is based on cognition and generates understanding. If clinical psychoanalytic formulations may be regarded as primarily dependent for their language and form on an empirical methodology, metapsychology, being more abstract, formal, and observation-distant, is essentially deductive. Both approaches may be regarded as scientific and therefore legitimate and worthy of serious attention. To illustrate the emphasis on its abstractness, Schmidt-Hellerau uses the analogy of Mandelbrot sets, the elaborate images generated by computers and based on simple mathematical formulas.

1021

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

JAPA: Ten Years

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards  

As I prepare to say farewell to the editorship of JAPA, I realize that I am much more aware than I was when I undertook the post what a monumental effort a journal like this is. Over the past ten years we have published forty issues and two supplements—13,359 pages of some of the finest psychoanalytic thought of our time. We have evaluated—read and carefully reviewed—almost 1500 submissions, some of them several times over. Throughout, we have striven to foster the creativity and generativity of our field, encouraging and reaching out to an ever broader community of readers and contributors. None of this happens without work, and a scholarly periodical of JAPA's caliber is the fruit of constant, intimate, and complicated collaboration. Our team starts with sixty elected members of the editorial board, twenty-eight editorial associates, seven associate editors, and two book review editors, and that is just the beginning.

Perhaps less obvious to our readers is the network of dedicated staff who work behind the scenes to produce, distribute, and market JAPA—our superb production team and manuscript editors, the secretarial assistants who provide endless and unstinting administrative support, and to varying degrees most of the administrative staff and board members of APsaA. Every single one of these people has had a hand in making JAPA what it is, and I have had to be forcibly restrained from naming each of them. Consideration of the sheer number of people it takes to make JAPA keeps an editor-in-chief from getting too cocky.

Still, since the name at the top of the masthead is mine, and since I'm speaking on behalf of my colleagues too, I feel entitled to crow a little. We are very proud of what we have accomplished, even if I must limit my bragging to just a few examples. Our first supplement, The Psychology of Women (44/Suppl.), coedited with Phyllis Tyson, sold out and had to go into a second printing. Published twenty years after the first supplement on the subject, it is still widely cited. Our second supplement, The Politics of Psychoanalysis (51/Suppl.)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Françoise Meltzer on Baudry's “Flaubert and Madame Bovary”

Françoise Meltzer

Françoise Meltzer

Wieboldt 205
1050 East 59th Street
Chicago, IL 60637
Email: Mltz@midway.uchicago.edu

Françoise Meltzer  

March 23, 2003. As psychoanalysis and literature continue their uneasy alliance, I read with interest Francis Baudry's “Flaubert and Madame Bovary: An Intimate Courtship” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.50/4, 2002). I think, however, that a few remarks from the field of literature need to be made, if only to clarify the difficulties inherent in looking at the literary text with psychoanalytic lenses.

Baudry would have us believe that Flaubert was able to “imagine himself in the persona of a woman” not only because he had the expected qualities of self-observation, empathy, identification, curiosity, and so on. More importantly for Baudry, Flaubert's remarkable ability to describe the “soul of a woman” is rooted in “very profound bisexual characteristics.” There is, I would like to submit, something wrong here: are we to believe, because of Molly's soliloquy in Ulysses, that Joyce was bisexual? Ah, you will say, his Nora helped him write that, hence its realistic tone. But Flaubert, as Baudry himself reminds us, had an intense correspondence with the writer Louise Colet during the difficult four and a half years of Bovary's composition. He wrote endlessly to her about the progress and details of the novel, and she to him. Isn't this a similar collaboration, if one wants to think in such causal terms? And by the way, why does Baudry refer to Colet as “a difficult, demanding, would-be writer”? Colet, largely forgotten as a writer today, was very famous in her day. She had won the Académie Française prize in poetry on numerous occasions—an honor by any standard. The fact that she has fallen by the wayside (along with teeming throngs of equally forgettable writers) does not make her a would-be writer: she wrote.

Bisexuality in itself, further, is not a passport to more insight into the opposite sex. George Eliot may have been self-described as “ugly,” but she was not bisexual, and the men in her novels seem nonetheless quite wonderfully believable. Oscar Wilde's Salome, and his version of her mother Herodias, do not give us an in-depth view into the “soul of a woman,” even though Wilde was bisexual. And what if a writer is strictly homosexual? Will he have less insight into women than a heterosexual male?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Women

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson

677 Turquoise Street.
La Jolla, CA 92037-1807
Fax: 619-452-1467
E-mail: Phyllis@tysonz.com

Phyllis Tyson  

This is the final issue of JAPA under the stewardship of Arnold D. Richards, and we owe him great appreciation for the work he has done to advance our Journal. We have seen JAPA's new breadth in interesting formats and broad-ranging discussions such as those earlier this year on neurobiology and on the promise for psychoanalysis of the bridges being built to other branches of science. But this issue returns to the consulting room.

Indeed, most of the papers in this issue emerge from efforts to further our understanding of the adult patients with whom we work—especially our understanding of women. The mapping of this “dark continent” has preoccupied and perplexed psychoanalysts for more than three-quarters of a century, with the result that the continent is no longer quite so dark. But a generally accepted, comprehensive, and integrated theory of female development, psychology, and sexuality has yet to emerge. Important controversies remain unresolved, and some areas are still incompletely explored, poorly understood, or in need of a fresh look. The papers in this issue provide that fresh look, and in so doing make an important contribution toward the continually evolving and changing theory of the psychology of the female.

Inability to master conflicts of aggression is a theme that permeates many of these studies. Maladaptive ways of expressing (or avoiding) feelings of anger, hate, rage, envy, competition and the like, and maladaptive methods of resolving interpersonal conflict, are common themes in the analyses of young girls and women. Many of these authors discuss how inadequate solutions of conflict, intrapsychic or interpersonal, interfere with a woman's ability to make career choices, find a successful blending of family and career, or deal appropriately with her children. An inability to assume agency over aggression can also impede her capacity for independent thinking, independent expression of her own opinions, and independent exertion of power and control in her life.

This remarkable book captures the scholarly attitude, attention to epistemic problems, and emphasis on critical thinking intrinsic to the Anna Freud Centre. The Centre has a long-standing tradition of theoretical and conceptual research based on study groups discussing clinical material. These groups find that “new clinical observations throw up new problems which require modification of existing theories or the development of new theoretical explanations. New theories in turn influence clinical understanding and technique” (Edgcumbe 2000, p. 79). More recently, Centre research analysts have been conducting empirical studies, including efforts to correlate and integrate psychoanalytic understanding with knowledge from other disciplines. In addition, there has been increasing interest in combining empirical methods with the work of clinical study groups and other qualitative methods.

Conversations at The Frontier of Dreaming. By Thomas H. Ogden. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2001, 257 pp. $40.00.

Henry Evans

Henry Evans

122 South Michigan Avenue
Suite 1411A
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: Hevans2073@aol.com

Henry Evans   Former Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Over the past twenty years or so, Thomas Ogden (1982; 1986; 1989; 1994a, b; 1997) has produced an influential series of works that have illuminated, altered, and extended Kleinian thinking. Recently he has been studying the means by which analyst and patient communicate and how therapeutic interaction and influence occur. He has argued (Ogden 1997) that the central aim of analysis is the patient's ability to experience more fully the broad spectrum of human emotions. Ogden believes this aim is best served by creating and maintaining affective immediacy between analyst and patient. Consistent with this, his exposition of his material allows readers to comprehend his clinical approach with a lived immediacy.

In Conversations at the Frontier of Dreaming, Ogden examines the essence of analytic sensibilities and therapeutic interaction. He explores the intricacies of language and the experience of conversations—with conversations referring to language-borne interactions that occur within ourselves, in the interchanges between analyst and patient, between the writer and the written, and between the written and the reader.

The conversations of greatest interest to Ogden concern a dialectic between the expressible and the inexpressible, an ongoing dynamic relationship between opposites that create, fulfill, negate, and preserve one another, each meaningful only in the context of the other, ever tending toward integration yet never achieving it (Ogden 1986, p. 208). The most familiar examples of this dialectic in psychoanalysis are Freud's conception of the interrelationship of conscious, preconscious, and unconscious mental activity (1915) and Winnicott's concept of transitional objects and phenomena. In Ogden's work, the dialectic is found in his concept of the inter subjective analytic third (1994a, 1994b), a dynamically unconscious, intersubjective state, co-constructed by analyst and patient but also reflecting their individual contributions.

Ogden's use of dialectic conversations is informed primarily by Freud's topographic model (1900, 1915). He uses Freud's “frontier” metaphor (1915, p. 193), focusing on the dialectic between preconscious and unconscious mental functioning. There Ogden finds “the very core of what it means to be alive as a human being” (p. 7).

Analysis Of The Under-Five Child. Edited by Robert L. Tyson. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2001, xv + 300 pp., $40.00.

Paula Atkeson

Paula Atkeson

3000 Connecticut Avenue NW
Washington, DC 20008-2529
Fax: 202-966-4899
Email: Paula@atkeson.net

Paula Atkeson   Training and Supervising Analyst in Adult and Child Analysis, Baltimore-Washington Institute for Psychoanalysis; Faculty, Clinical Social Work Institute, Washington, DC.

It is not only the parents of an under-five child who respond with disbelief when psychoanalysis is recommended for their child, asking how their young child could be so disturbed as to need so much treatment for so long; child analysts themselves may be hesitant to make the recommendation for analysis, hoping that a less intensive approach will be sufficient. Many child analysts today have limited analytic experience with prelatency children, and other mental health professionals generally have had little exposure to the benefits of psychoanalytic treatment for the very young.

Robert L. Tyson, the editor of Analysis of the Under-Five Child, sets forth in his introduction the major historical, conceptual, and educational factors that have hindered a wider acceptance of psychoanalysis for those young children whose difficulties make this the treatment of choice. Citing the pleasures of analytic work with prelatency children—which include immersion in early developmental processes as well as frequent favorable outcomes that may be accomplished more rapidly than at later ages—Tyson hopes that this volume will contribute to establishing the years before five as “the golden age for a psychoanalysis” (p. xv).

Analysis of the Under-Five Child masterfully fulfills this goal. The heart of the book is the detailed presentation of eight beautifully described clinical cases of children who began their analyses at ages ranging from two years and nine months to four and a half years old. These cases convincingly demonstrate the unique contributions of the psychoanalytic process to the developmental progress of each young patient. Readers will appreciate the clinical skill, conceptual rigor, and analytic care with which the treatments are conducted, as well as the analysts' attention to the uniqueness of each child. Formal discussions of several of the cases expand theoretical and clinical facets of the work, and conceptual perspectives are provided in two final discussions focusing on psychoanalysis and development, and psychoanalytic technique with the young child.

Dori Laub   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine; cofounder, Fortunoff Video Archive for Holocaust Testimonies.

While Ira Brenner's writing on phenomenology and technique flows naturally here, and is masterful, his struggles with psychoanalytic theory seem almost Herculean. Brenner himself seems aware of this: “I do not think that a full understanding of DID [Dissociative Identity Disorder] can occur until Freud's goal of uniting dream psychology and psychopathology is realized” (p. 35). The struggle begins with Brenner's stance toward external reality, in which he practically reverses the shift Freud made in 1897 from the seduction theory to the idea of infantile sexuality: “Given the problems of psychic reality, memory, and reconstruction—especially in situations of physical and sexual abuse—I have included only those cases when there was corroboration of the past” (p. ix). Almost all the case studies in the book are indeed about women who experienced incest in their early childhood, often in addition to other forms of sexual and physical abuse.

The “real” massive trauma is experienced by the child defensively in a dissociated ego state, which is the origin of both (a) the post-traumatic dream, in which the identities of perpetrator and victim are disguised, and (b) the dissociated personifications, in which two categories of identities are clear but a “pseudoexternalized displacement” is employed “in the service of negation” (p. 90). The plot of the abuse is the same, however, in the manifest traumatic dream and in the memories of the “alter.” The dissociated personifications remember and re-enact the plot and its derivatives, within and without the transference situation, as having happened or happening to “someone else” (p. 87).

669

Dangerous Behavior in Children and Adolescents

Dominic Mazza

Dominic Mazza

500 Wheeler Avenue
Scranton, PA 18510

Dominic Mazza  

In the aftermath of the recent episodes of school shootings, the most notorious being the massacre at Columbine High School in Colorado, increased media and medical attention has been focused on violent children. The panel's chair, Judith Chused, reminded the audience that beginning with the seminal work of August Aichhorn, adolescents and younger children who demonstrate dangerous and violent behaviors have long been of interest to psychoanalysts. Chused briefly described her experiences treating young patients who manifest their psychological distress via substance abuse, promiscuity, antisocial behaviors, and physical violence. She stressed the difficulties of forging a working/therapeutic alliance with patients whose narcissistic vulnerabilities and history of insecure attachments impel action and not introspection, and whose psychic equilibrium is threatened by the emergence of libidinal longings within the transference.

Particular countertransference reactions stimulated by these patients—e.g., the therapist's anxiety about the patient's safety outside the office (which might threaten confidentiality), or anxiety about therapist and/or patient safety within the sessions (which might threaten the analyst's abstinent posture)—were described by Chused. She stressed the importance of establishing clear limits and boundaries to ensure an ambience conducive to analytic work and the development of analyzable transferences. The centrality of limits and boundaries to the successful treatment of these children was a point stressed by each of the panelists.

Before introducing the presenters, Chused outlined some of the important biological, psychological, and environmental factors that contribute to a patient's propensity for violent behavior.

Panel held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 16, 2000. Panelists: Judith Fingert Chused (chair), Phyllis Tyson, Steven Marans, Stuart W. Twemlow.

[[RunningHead]]

Vicissitudes of Aggression

Theoretical and Technical Approaches to Psychic Trauma

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10021
Email: 73542.334@compuserve.com

Leon Hoffman  

It is now almost fifty years since Leo Stone (1954) wrote his classic paper, “The Widening Scope of Indications for Psychoanalysis.” As readers of JAPA, particularly during the recent past, we have been privileged to learn more about this broadened reach of psychoanalytic ideas. This issue, with its wealth of clinical material, focuses on the value of psychoanalytic principles, old and new, in understanding and treating very difficult patients, particularly those who have undergone severely traumatic experiences.

Encountering another's clinical material, particularly when it involves complex clinical situations, leads inevitably to an influence (conscious and unconscious) on our own clinical work, as we compare and contrast the other's theory and technique with our own. Are they consonant with each other or divergent? Which approach is more consonant with our actual practice? Is the novel theoretical construction helpful in organizing our own clinical data? Studying the difficult clinical situations described in this issue, and the theoretical discussion they inspire, can help all of us apply the successes (and failures) of these authors to our own clinical work.

In 1927 Freud described the nonpsychotic disavowal of reality of two young men who had lost their fathers in childhood. In these men, two mental states existed side by side: one comported with a wish, the other with reality. In the posthumously published “An Outline of Psycho-analysis,” Freud (1940) discussed further the idea that splitting, as a mechanism, is ubiquitous in childhood. Children, he stated, in addition to coping with undesirable instinctual demands by repression, often find themselves needing to fend off distressing demands from the external world.

Happiness, Death and the Remainder of Life. By Jonathan Lear. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2000, 224 pp., $26.00 hardcover, $16.00 paperback.

Maurice Apprey

Maurice Apprey

2805 Brookmere Road
Charlottesville, VA 22901-1108
Fax: 804-982-4073
E-mail: Ma9h@virginia.edu

Maurice Apprey   Professor of Psychiatry and Associate Dean, University of Virginia School of Medicine.

The work of Jonathan Lear is a serious attempt at integration between two disciplines, Anglo-American analytic philosophy and psychoanalysis. Finding a synthesis between the traditions is part of his own development as a practitioner of both disciplines. However, the form of integration put forward here is not simply a juxtaposition of two grand traditions. Rather, it is an attempt to find what is missing in each tradition before new and possible solutions can be found. In this respect, synthesis is transformation in Lear's work. This is an ambitious and legitimate project and Lear is up to it. The problem for this reviewer is whether he is going to be bold enough. Let us now go to the pretext, context, and text of Lear's new book, Happiness, Death and the Remainder of Life.

It was written in response to the invitation to give the Tanner Lectures on Human Values at the University of Cambridge, United Kingdom, in the academic year 1999-2000. This context as to where and why these lectures were given is important to Lear. Cambridge was the place where he discovered that philosophy was to be an active, integral part of his life; it was not simply the product of what other scholars thought. There, he discovered that philosophy was “an activity of the soul” (p. 184).

Context and pretext are, however, connected, and we get a glimpse of their implicit interdigitation when Lear pays a debt to Jacques Lacan and Jean Laplanche as follows: “We owe them the idea that the history of psychoanalysis can be read as a history of the resistance to its own insight” (p. 167). I foreground this footnote buried at the back of the book to make more explicit Lear's pretext and project, which he began to develop in his previous work, Open Minded (1999), and here expands on and extends.

349

Samuel Gerson

Samuel Gerson

2252 Fillmore Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
E-mail: Samgerson@aol.com

Samuel Gerson   Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

It is common knowledge that supervision is a form of practice that is typically undertaken in the absence of a thought-through approach, let alone formal training. Moreover, a large percentage of supervisors begin and continue their work without so much as a cursory reading of the literature. While a few concepts may have passed into the general vocabulary (“parallel process,” for instance), supervisors are most often without any conceptual framework that may both guide the work and offer a reflective vantage point. As a result, most of us are left with a model based only on our own personal experiences of being supervised. In the absence of alternative models that include the insights of more recent developments in theory and practice, our dated experience is generally replicated through identifications. It is most fortunate that we now have a book that addresses the gaps in a manner that is simultaneously scholarly and practical. The Supervisory Relationship: A Contemporary Psychodynamic Approach, by Mary Gail Frawley-O'Dea and Joan Sarnat, extends the intellectual vigor and clinical relevance of our multiple ways of knowing to the supervisory process in a manner that is accessible, thought-provoking, and immediately useful.

The opening review of previous work about supervision is both excellent for its comprehensiveness and original in its placement of the supervisory process within developments in the broader sociologies of power and knowledge that saturate psychoanalytic history.

345

Can psychoanalysis, addressing human nature generally, help establish a universal ground for morality, which could apply across varying historical and sociocultural conditions? George Frankl admirably has undertaken a broad exploration and synthesis of such questions, turning not only to psychoanalysis, but also to neuroscience, archaeology, and philosophy.

Aggression and Women

Ruth Garfield

Ruth Garfield

355D Lancaster Avenue
Haverford, PA 19041
E-mail: Ruthgmd@aol.com

Ruth Garfield  

Ruth Fischer, who chaired the panel, began by reviewing recent milestones in psychoanalytic thinking about female development. Robert Stoller's concept of primary femininity, she said, sharpened the intuitive sense held by many analysts that the experience of femaleness is specif ic and essential, that it is the absence or otherness of maleness but has its own def inition. Since Stoller began his work, a substantial body of literature has elaborated and advanced his ideas.

Very little, however, has been written about the place of aggression in a woman's experience. The concept of aggression, ungendered, presents difficulties. On the one hand, aggression in its more “positive” template appears as ambition, curiosity, and assertiveness. On the other hand, aggression is often synonymous with hostility or violence. In the psychoanalytic literature, aggression is considered instinctual, but questions arise about the inf luence of “nurture” and culture on the expression that aggression takes. These questions encompass the role of gender in the experience and expression of aggression. First arises the basic question of whether there are differences for men and women in aggression. If so, what accounts for these? Are such differences determined biologically, psychologically, or culturally?

It is common to hear that women shrink from aggressive experiences, competitiveness, and assertiveness. In her studies, Carol Gilligan reports that the self-esteem and assertiveness of girls diminish as they proceed through adolescence. These troubling tendencies alert us to the need to understand the inhibition of aggression in women. It is common also to hear about women whose control over aggressive impulses is sorely def icient. The patients we diagnose as borderline are largely women who exhibit problems with aggression. Clearly, neither inhibition nor poor impulse control of aggression is limited to

responseSet.title: On the Question of the Patient's Right to Tell and the Ethical Reality of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Question of the Patient's Right to Tell and the Ethical Reality of Psychoanalysis

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

Mount Scopus, Jerusalem
ISRAEL, 91905
Fax: 972-2-588-1159
E-mail: Rblass@mscc.huji.ac.il

Rachel Blass   Head, Clinical Psychology Faction, and Senior Lecturer, Department of Psychology, Hebrew University of Jerusalem; Candidate, Israel Psychoanalytic Institute.

This paper calls into question the view that it is ethically legitimate for the patient to say whatever comes to his or her mind: that is, to adhere to the fundamental rule. While there have been some variations in the application of this rule since Freud's time, it remains for many the bedrock of clinical practice, and the patient's right to free-associate has never been questioned. Recent debates on the importance of the analyst's strict confidentiality have highlighted this right. Ethical problems raised by adherence to the fundamental rule are explored through an examination of the general ethical limitations on what one may say to another person, and the special features of the analytic relationship that seem to do away with these limitations. The fact that there are ethical questions about adherence to the fundamental rule draws attention to what the author calls the ethical reality of psychoanalysis. The recognition of this reality has implications for the understanding and handling of ethical dilemmas regarding disclosure, as well as for other ethical issues that may arise in the course of an analysis.

responseSet.title: Standards and Standardization

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Standards and Standardization

Owen Renik

Owen Renik

388 Market Street
Suite 1010
San Francisco, CA 94111
E-mail: Odrenik@aol.com

Owen Renik  

I'm quite sure that every one of us is convinced of the necessity to formulate and maintain psychoanalytic standards; further, I think we all agree that analysts should be required to conform, to the best of their ability, to a standard of excellence in psychoanalytic work. That said, however, we face a monumental problem because—as we well know—there is no consensus among us as to the nature of excellence in psychoanalytic work. What some of us will judge to be an analyst's creative and useful technical innovation, others of us will consider irresponsible self-indulgence on the analyst's part; what some of us will regard as an analyst's advisable caution and restraint, others of us will see as the analyst's self-protective inhibition; what impresses some of us as sensitive interpretation of the patient's here-and-now experience, others will see as an avoidance of the transference relationship and an impediment to the unfolding of the patient's unconscious conflicts; what to some will seem a timely reconstruction, others will perceive as an invitation to intellectualize and ruminate about the past; and so on. It would be comforting to believe that we can formulate a standard of excellence that transcends our individual assumptions, one that can be applied to all psychoanalysts, across our very different operating theories. But, alas, no such standard is possible because our judgments of excellence are inextricably tied to our widely varying, often conflicting conceptions of the psychoanalytic enterprise.

The actual situation in our field is aptly captured, in my opinion, by the well-known anecdote that concerns a meeting of the British Psychoanalytical Society at which Wilfred Bion was the presenter. Bion, as you may know, besides being a renowned psychoanalytic contributor, was a decorated military hero during the First World War.

Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Professor of Psychiatry, University of California Medical Center at San Francisco.

[[RunningHead]]

Interpreting Mitchell's Constructivism

Reply to Altman and Davies

Morris Eagle , Jerome Wakefield and David Wolitzky

David Wolitzky

Garden City, NY 11530
Fax: 516-877-4805
E-mail: Meagle100@aol.com

I Washington Square North
New York, NY 10003
Fax: 212-222-9524
E-mail: Jerome.Wakefield@nyu.edu

New York NY 10012
Fax: 212-995-4292
E-mail: Dwolitzky@aol.com

David Wolitzky  

We will respond to Altman and Davies's specific criticisms of the arguments in our paper (Eagle, Wolitzky, and Wakefield 2001), in which we analyzed and critiqued some of the philosophical assumptions underlying Stephen Mitchell's and Owen Renik's constructivist accounts of psychoanalytic interpretation. First, however, we must comment on an issue far more important than the validity of this or that specific argument or theoretical point. Twice in their paper, Altman and Davies imply that our paper so obviously and egregiously distorts Mitchell's ideas that, as editor, Arnold Richards should have refused to publish it. They ask him “whether he believes that the articles which he has chosen to publish [particularly ours and another by Jay Greenberg] conform to his own standards for discourse” as outlined in his article in this issue (emphasis added). Later, more explicitly, they write, “Richards's willingness to publish this article, which so distorts the essence of Mitchell's argument, seems to us at sharp variance with the plea for respectful dialogue he presents …” (emphasis added). Altman and Davies make this chilling suggestion despite the fact that, as they must know, the paper was peer reviewed and recommended for publication. It would be amusing, if it weren't so serious, that in a paper titled “A Plea for Constructive Dialogue” it is suggested that an editor should

responseSet.title: Transference: Shibboleth or Albatross?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference: Shibboleth or Albatross?

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter

160 West 66th Street
New York, NY 10023-6555
E-mail: Jschachter2@nyc.rr.com

Joseph Schachter  

July 8, 2003. I would like to thank David Wolitzky for adding a positive view of Transference: Shibboleth or Albatross? (JAPA 51/2) to the positive evaluations of Eagle and Gross. Wolitzky agrees that I am “probably correct in claiming that analysts overstate the case for continuity based on early experience” and concludes that “this book succeeds admirably in clearly dispelling any simplistic, reductionistic, or linear notions of psychological causality” (pp. 713, 717). However, I do not accept some specific criticisms, quoted below, a few of which seem gratuitous.

“He believes that a focus on the here-and-now transference ‘may throw light, by extrapolation into the past, on the meaning of earlier experiences, including those of childhood, rather than the other way around.’ It is not clear what Schachter means by this” (p. 715).

My emphasis is not on the here-and-now transference but rather on here-and-now patient-analyst interactions. The point of my book is that these are different. Transference intrinsically involves the search for past origins; examining patient-analyst interactions eschews a search for past origins and substitutes exploration of the patient's present unconscious to elucidate the unconscious fantasies and defenses that influence the patient's behavior in the interactions. If specific conflicts do not emerge in patient-analyst interactions, they certainly must be explored in relationships outside the office. I accept that some patients do seem to be helped by “transference” interpretations—and others do not—though the role of suggestion in the therapeutic effect always has to be considered.

Let me explain again, as I do on pages 152, 153, and 179, about extrapolation to earlier figures. The one person about whom the patient reports interactions and responses, about whom the analyst has the greatest amount of valid data regarding the nature and behavior of that person, is the self-observant analyst him- or herself. Analysts have direct access to knowing not only how they behaved with a patient, but also what they were feeling about the patient at the time of the interaction.

responseSet.title: The Holding Function of Theory
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Holding Function of Theory

Richard Almond

Richard Almond

550 Hamilton Avenue, Suite 339
Palo Alto, CA 94301
E-mail: Rjalmond@leland.stanford.edu

Richard Almond   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Science, Stanford University School of Medicine.

As multiple theoretical models contend on the American analytic scene, the holding function of theory emerges as a unifying theme. In addition to supplying an intellectual superstructure for the working analyst, theory provides a psychological presence—a sense of conviction, affective stability, reassurance, and self-esteem— that makes effective analytic work possible from the analyst's side. Ideological passions and differences arise from the vital need for the holding function in an intense and inchoate engagement like psychoanalysis. To show how adherents of different models use theory in practice, three clinical cases are reviewed, one from Betty Joseph, one from Lewis Aron, and one from the author. The last example reconstructs the analyst's subjective experience of treatment both in the selected hours and in terms of the analyst's preconscious use of theory.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Discourse at the Turn of Our Century: A Plea for a Measure of Humility
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Discourse at the Turn of Our Century: A Plea for a Measure of Humility

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: Arniedr@aol.com

Arnold Richards  

Since Freud's death in 1939, American psychoanalysis has grown from a small, homogeneous, and hierarchical community that resolved disputes primarily by ostracizing dissidents to a larger and more democratic one notable for its intellectual and cultural diversity. Theoretical pluralism and an increasingly heterogeneous population of practitioners have precipitated a renewed struggle for equilibrium between subversive energies and older traditions and have tested the civility of our discourse as perhaps never before. Although these challenges should not be underestimated, the present moment affords analysts in the United States a unique opportunity for mending fences and building bridges. To this end, I would urge adherents of the various analytic traditions to forgo a discourse of dichotomy and polarization and instead approach one another in a dialectical spirit. Just as the essence of science is to be open to evidence that might refute one's preconceptions, so too is it the essence of the psychoanalytic wisdom to which we all aspire to avoid defensiveness and to acknowledge our mistakes. Human nature being what it is, a counsel of perfection is not what I have in mind; a plea for a measure of humility on all sides, however, seems eminently worth making.

This paper was presented in the Distinguished Lecturer Series, Contemporary Center for Advanced Psychoanalytic Studies, Fairleigh Dickinson University, October 26, 2003. Submitted for publication May 2, 2001.

73

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Astonishing Clinical Irrelevance of Neuroscience

Sydney Pulver

Sydney Pulver

111 North 49th Street
Philadelphia, PA 19139
Fax: 215-472-7716
Email: Spulver@mail.med.upenn.edu

Sydney Pulver   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Pennsylvania Medical School.

The clinical work of psychoanalysts can be thought of in both a narrow and a broad sense. In the narrow sense, it refers to what is commonly thought of as psychoanalytic technique, the methods analysts use to understand their patients and to convey that understanding to them. In the broad sense, it refers to the entirety of their work as clinicians: the content of understanding, as well as such things as diagnosing, estimating analyzability, recommending therapy, and prescribing medication. The current enthusiastic expectation that neuroscience will have an immediate and direct impact on clinical work in the narrow sense is misguided, but neuroscientific discoveries, it is argued, will have a major impact on psychoanalytic theory in the not too distant future. The resulting changes in metapsychology will ultimately have reverberations on clinical work in the broad sense, although psychoanalytic technique, the analyst's basic approach to patients, will remain essentially the same.
